proposals.title: Development and Validation of a Standardized, Competency-Based Education Program for Insulin Pump and Continuous Glucose Monitor Initiation in Community Pharmacy Settings
proposals.motivation: The rapid proliferation of complex insulin delivery technologies (e.g., insulin pumps, hybrid closed-loop systems, continuous glucose monitors) has outpaced the development of standardized, accessible patient education protocols. Pre-2026 evidence indicates that inconsistent or inadequate education is a key barrier to optimal device utilization, leading to poor glycemic control, device discontinuation, and increased risk of adverse events. Community pharmacists, as highly accessible healthcare providers, are uniquely positioned to deliver this education but require structured, evidence-based tools to do so effectively.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a structured, competency-based education program for new insulin delivery technologies, delivered by community pharmacists, will lead to superior patient outcomes—including improved glycemic control (HbA1c), higher device self-efficacy, and greater long-term adherence—compared to usual manufacturer-provided or ad-hoc education at 6 and 12 months post-initiation.
proposals.method: This will be a multi-phase, mixed-methods study. First, a Delphi panel of endocrinologists, certified diabetes care and education specialists, and expert pharmacists will iteratively develop a core competency framework and a standardized education curriculum. This curriculum will then be operationalized into a modular, multimedia training program for pharmacists. In the validation phase, a pragmatic, cluster-randomized controlled trial will be conducted across 30 community pharmacies. Pharmacies will be randomized to implement the new program or continue usual care. Patient outcomes (HbA1c, device adherence metrics from downloads, validated self-efficacy scales) will be compared between groups at baseline, 3, 6, and 12 months using linear mixed-effects models to account for clustering.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorously developed and validated, standardized education protocol for advanced diabetes technology that is specifically designed for the community pharmacy workflow. It will generate high-level evidence on the long-term clinical and humanistic impact of structured pharmacist-led device education, directly informing best practice guidelines. The resulting toolkit will be freely disseminated to enhance the capacity of the pharmacy workforce in diabetes care.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have evaluated pharmacist education programs, none have pre-2026 developed a national competency standard and a corresponding validated curriculum specifically for the initiation phase of complex insulin delivery devices, nor tested its long-term efficacy in a real-world, community-based randomized trial.

proposals.title: Comparative Transferability of Success Factors to Ambulatory Care: A Mixed-Methods Analysis of Retail Pharmacy and Digital Health Models in Diabetes Management
proposals.motivation: Ambulatory care pharmacy is a critical setting for managing chronic diseases like diabetes, but its expansion is resource-intensive. Meanwhile, retail pharmacy and digital health have developed scalable models for patient engagement and monitoring. Determining which success factors are most transferable can guide efficient practice transformation and improve patient outcomes in ambulatory settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that structural and process factors from digital health models (e.g., asynchronous monitoring, data-driven alerts) will be more readily transferable to ambulatory care pharmacy than factors from retail pharmacy models, due to greater alignment in care objectives and workflow integration potential.
proposals.method: We will conduct a sequential explanatory mixed-methods study. First, a systematic literature review (2015-2025) will identify documented success factors in retail and digital health diabetes management. Second, we will perform a quantitative survey of ambulatory care pharmacists to rate the perceived transferability of each factor using a validated instrument. Finally, semi-structured interviews with key informants will explore barriers and facilitators to implementing the highest-rated factors. Data will be integrated to create a prioritized transferability framework.
proposals.contribution: This study will produce a novel, evidence-based framework ranking the transferability of success factors from alternative care models into ambulatory pharmacy. It will provide actionable guidance for health systems and educators seeking to efficiently develop or expand ambulatory care services, directly impacting diabetes care delivery and pharmacist training programs.
proposals.novelty: While prior work compares outcomes across models, this study uniquely focuses on the *transferability* of underlying operational and engagement factors, moving beyond efficacy to address practical implementation. It directly tests the assumption that ambulatory care is the primary source of its own best practices.

proposals.title: Balancing Act: A Mixed-Methods Study on the Association Between Expanded Clinical Services and Dispensing Error Rates in Community Pharmacies
proposals.motivation: The expansion of pharmacist roles into clinical services (e.g., diabetes education, point-of-care testing, chronic disease management) is a central theme in pharmacy practice evolution. However, pre-2026 literature largely assumes this expansion is uniformly beneficial, with limited investigation into potential unintended consequences. It is critical to understand if increased clinical workload impacts the safety and efficiency of pharmacists' core dispensing duties, which remain fundamental to patient care.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in community pharmacy settings, a higher volume of delivered clinical services is associated with an increased rate of dispensing errors and workflow interruptions, particularly during peak operational hours and in pharmacies with lower staffing ratios.
proposals.method: This will be a prospective, observational cohort study using a convergent mixed-methods design. Quantitatively, we will recruit 50 community pharmacies to report monthly metrics on clinical service volume (e.g., medication therapy management encounters, immunizations, diabetes education sessions) and dispensing error rates (via standardized incident reports) over a 12-month period. Data will be analyzed using multivariable Poisson regression, controlling for pharmacy characteristics (staffing, prescription volume). Qualitatively, semi-structured interviews and focus groups with pharmacists and technicians from a subset of sites will explore perceived workflow disruptions and safety trade-offs, with thematic analysis used to identify key mechanisms.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal, multi-site evidence directly linking clinical service expansion to potential risks in dispensing safety. It will generate a nuanced framework for understanding the 'tipping point' where benefits of role expansion may be offset by safety concerns, informing the development of optimized workflow models and staffing guidelines to support integrated care safely.
proposals.novelty: While existing research by Morello and others demonstrates the positive outcomes of clinical programs, this proposal directly tests the counter-hypothesis that such expansion may have negative downstream effects on core pharmacy operations, a trade-off rarely examined in the pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Evaluating the Implementation of a Diabetes Self-Care Education Program in Community Pharmacies Using the RE-AIM Framework: A Mixed-Methods Study
proposals.motivation: Community pharmacies are highly accessible points of care, yet the systematic implementation of evidence-based clinical services, like diabetes self-management education (DSME), remains inconsistent. This gap contributes to suboptimal patient outcomes and health disparities. Implementation science frameworks, such as RE-AIM, provide a structured approach to evaluate and improve the real-world adoption of such services, but their application in independent community pharmacy settings is understudied.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a structured implementation strategy guided by the RE-AIM framework will lead to greater reach, adoption, and maintenance of a pharmacist-led DSME program in community pharmacies, compared to usual dissemination methods, and will be associated with improved patient-level clinical outcomes over a 12-month period.
proposals.method: This will be a 12-month, multi-site, mixed-methods study. A cluster-randomized design will assign community pharmacies to an implementation group (receiving a RE-AIM-guided toolkit, training, and facilitation) or a control group (receiving program materials only). Quantitative data (patient HbA1c, program participation rates, pharmacist surveys) will be collected at baseline, 6, and 12 months. Qualitative data from pharmacist interviews will explore implementation barriers and facilitators. Analysis will compare between-group differences in RE-AIM dimensions (Reach, Effectiveness, Adoption, Implementation, Maintenance) using mixed-effects models.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a practical, framework-driven model for scaling clinical services in community pharmacy, a critical but underutilized healthcare setting. It will generate actionable evidence on the specific facilitators and barriers to sustaining DSME, contributing directly to the literature on pharmacy practice transformation and implementation science in real-world community contexts.
proposals.novelty: While RE-AIM has been used in health services research, its deliberate application to guide and evaluate the *process* of implementing a sustained clinical education program within the independent community pharmacy ecosystem is novel. This work integrates Dr. Morello's expertise in long-term program evaluation and diabetes with a rigorous implementation science methodology not commonly applied in this specific practice setting.

proposals.title: Designing and Validating a Sustainable Community Pharmacy Diabetes Management Service Using the Business Model Canvas
proposals.motivation: Community pharmacies are uniquely positioned to provide accessible, long-term care for chronic conditions like diabetes, yet many clinical service programs fail due to a lack of sustainable business models. Prior to 2026, research by Morello and others demonstrates the clinical efficacy of pharmacist-led diabetes education but highlights challenges in program longevity and integration into routine practice. A structured approach to designing viable service offerings is urgently needed to translate proven clinical benefits into real-world, sustained impact.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying the Business Model Canvas (BMC) framework to co-design a community pharmacy-based diabetes management service will result in a more operationally and financially sustainable service model compared to traditional, clinically-focused program design alone.
proposals.method: This mixed-methods study will first conduct qualitative interviews and focus groups with community pharmacists, patients with diabetes, and payers to identify key value propositions and operational challenges. These insights will be used to iteratively populate the nine building blocks of the BMC (e.g., Key Partners, Revenue Streams, Cost Structure) for a proposed service. The designed BMC will then be implemented as a pilot program in 10-15 community pharmacies for a 12-month period. Key metrics for comparison between baseline and follow-up will include service adoption rates, pharmacist-reported sustainability, patient retention, and revenue generated, analyzed using paired t-tests and thematic analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, practical framework for community pharmacies to systematically develop financially viable clinical services, moving beyond proof-of-efficacy studies. It will contribute a validated BMC template for diabetes management that can be adapted for other chronic conditions, directly addressing the gap between clinical evidence and sustainable practice implementation in community pharmacy.
proposals.novelty: While the BMC is established in business literature, its structured application to co-design and empirically test the sustainability of a specific community pharmacy clinical service is novel. This integrates business model innovation directly into patient care research, a non-obvious synthesis of Morello's prior work on long-term program impact and holistic pharmacy practice.

proposals.title: Calibrating Self-Reported Medication Adherence in Diabetes Using Ecological Momentary Assessment and Objective Data: A Validation and Contextual Analysis Study
proposals.motivation: Self-reported medication adherence is a cornerstone of clinical management and research in diabetes, yet its validity is often questioned due to recall bias and social desirability. This inaccuracy undermines clinical decision-making and the reliability of long-term studies, such as those investigating metformin's long-term effects. An objective, yet scalable, method to determine when self-reports are trustworthy is critically needed to improve patient monitoring and outcomes research.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that real-time, context-tagged self-reports of medication-taking collected via Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) can be statistically calibrated against objective adherence data to identify specific patient and contextual conditions under which self-reported adherence is a valid predictor of objective adherence and, subsequently, glycemic control (HbA1c).
proposals.method: We will recruit patients with type 2 diabetes on oral medication for a 4-week observational study. Participants will use a smartphone app for EMA, providing prompted and event-contingent reports of medication-taking with context tags (time, location, social setting). Objective adherence will be measured via electronic pill bottle monitors (e.g., MEMS Caps). We will employ multivariate regression and machine learning calibration models (e.g., gradient boosting) to predict objective adherence from EMA reports and contextual features. The model will identify the combinations of factors (e.g., time of day, patient confidence from prior education) that maximize prediction accuracy.
proposals.contribution: This study will produce a validated, context-aware framework for interpreting self-reported adherence in diabetes care, moving beyond a simple 'accurate vs. inaccurate' dichotomy. It will provide clinicians and researchers with evidence-based guidance on when to trust self-report data and could lead to a refined, calibrated self-report instrument for use in settings where continuous objective monitoring is not feasible.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in adapting calibration methodologies from environmental psychology to a clinical pharmacy context, specifically targeting the long-standing problem of adherence measurement. It moves from comparing aggregate measures to modeling the dynamic, contextual conditions of reporting validity, which has not been systematically done in diabetes adherence research pre-2026.

proposals.title: Disentangling the Components of Pharmacist-Led Care: A Randomized Controlled Trial Comparing Clinical Pharmacist Intervention to Standardized Health Coaching in Patients with Type 2 Diabetes
proposals.motivation: Pharmacist-led interventions consistently improve outcomes in chronic disease management, such as diabetes. However, it remains unclear whether the benefits are driven by the pharmacist's unique clinical expertise (e.g., medication optimization, safety monitoring) or by the non-specific effects of structured, personalized attention, which could be delivered by other trained personnel. Distinguishing these components is critical for optimizing care models and resource allocation in primary care and community pharmacy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a clinical pharmacist-led intervention will result in greater improvement in glycemic control (HbA1c) and medication adherence over a 12-month period compared to an attention-matched health coaching program, after controlling for baseline characteristics.
proposals.method: We propose a pragmatic, two-arm, parallel-group randomized controlled trial. Patients with uncontrolled type 2 diabetes will be randomized to either: 1) a clinical pharmacist intervention (medication therapy management, comprehensive reviews, deprescribing) or 2) a standardized health coaching program (focused on lifestyle, general adherence support, and active listening without clinical recommendations). Both groups will receive an equal number and duration of contacts over 12 months. The primary outcome is the change in HbA1c from baseline to 12 months, analyzed using a linear mixed-effects model with repeated measures to compare the trajectories between groups.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide direct, empirical evidence quantifying the value-added of clinical pharmacist expertise beyond generalized supportive care. The findings will inform health system leaders and payers on how to structure team-based care for chronic disease. It will also refine the theoretical framework for evaluating complex clinical interventions by isolating a key active ingredient.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies compare pharmacist care to usual care, this design innovatively controls for the 'dose' of personalized attention, using an active comparator to isolate the effect of clinical expertise—a methodological advance in pharmacy practice research.

proposals.title: Comparative Effectiveness of Proactive Identification Versus Standard Referral for a Pharmacist-Led Diabetes Prevention Program in a Community Pharmacy Setting
proposals.motivation: Diabetes prevention programs (DPPs) are effective but often rely on provider referrals, which may miss at-risk individuals not actively engaged in care. This passive enrollment model limits population health impact. Pharmacists are accessible healthcare providers well-positioned to proactively identify and manage at-risk patients, but the comparative effectiveness of proactive identification versus standard referral remains unquantified.
proposals.hypothesis: Proactive identification of at-risk patients using a validated risk score and targeted outreach will yield a higher enrollment rate and greater improvement in glycemic and cardiovascular risk markers over 12 months, compared to a standard referral-based model for a pharmacist-led DPP.
proposals.method: We will conduct a pragmatic, cluster-randomized trial across 20 community pharmacies. Pharmacies will be randomized to either a Proactive Identification arm or a Standard Referral arm. In the Proactive arm, pharmacists will use an electronic health record (EHR)-integrated algorithm (e.g., the CDC Prediabetes Screening Score or ADA Risk Test) to systematically identify eligible patients (aged ≥18, with prediabetes or high diabetes risk, not diagnosed with diabetes). Identified patients will receive a structured outreach protocol. In the Referral arm, enrollment will depend on external provider referrals. Both arms will implement the same 12-month, evidence-based pharmacist-led DPP. Primary outcomes will be program enrollment rate and change in HbA1c at 12 months. Secondary outcomes include changes in weight, fasting glucose, and patient-reported measures of self-efficacy.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust, comparative evidence on the population health impact of different patient identification strategies for pharmacist-led preventive services. It will inform healthcare systems and payers on optimal implementation models for scaling DPPs. The findings will directly extend the applicant's work on long-term program impact and pharmacist education in diabetes care.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have evaluated DPP effectiveness or pharmacist roles, this proposal uniquely isolates and tests the 'identification and enrollment' mechanism itself as a critical variable for population impact, moving beyond the assumption that an effective program model is sufficient. It applies a comparative effectiveness framework to a foundational implementation question in community pharmacy practice.

proposals.title: Mechanistic Drivers of Long-Term Glycemic Control: A 24-Month Mixed-Methods Cohort Study with Triggered In-Depth Analysis of Adherence Decline
proposals.motivation: Long-term sustainability of glycemic control remains a critical challenge in diabetes management, as many interventions show initial success that wanes over time. Pre-2026, there is a lack of granular, longitudinal data integrating behavioral, social, and clinical mechanisms to explain why some patients maintain improvements while others decline, limiting the design of durable support programs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, time-varying interactions between behavioral (e.g., self-efficacy), social (e.g., support network stability), and clinical (e.g., medication side effects) mechanisms will predict distinct trajectories of glycemic control sustainability over 24 months, and that targeted analysis of decline events will reveal modifiable, real-time barriers.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 24-month prospective cohort study of 300 adults with type 2 diabetes who have completed a structured education/support program. Quantitative data (HbA1c, ecological momentary assessments of barriers/confidence, pharmacy refill adherence) will be collected at 0, 6, 12, 18, and 24 months. Trajectory analysis (e.g., group-based trajectory modeling) will map sustainability paths. A pre-defined trigger (e.g., ≥1.5% rise in HbA1c from nadir) will enroll declining participants into an embedded mixed-methods sub-study, involving serial qualitative interviews and detailed contextual analysis to elucidate mechanisms of decline.
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond simply measuring durability to provide a mechanistic, longitudinal model of sustainability in diabetes self-management. It will generate actionable insights into the real-time factors precipitating decline, directly informing the design of adaptive, timely 'rescue' interventions within pharmacy-based care models.
proposals.novelty: The key innovation is the triggered, embedded sub-study protocol, which transforms the observation of decline into an active mechanism-learning opportunity, combining intensive mixed-methods data capture with targeted support in real time—a design not commonly employed in pre-2026 pharmacy practice research.

proposals.title: Sustained Impact of a Pharmacist-Led Diabetes Self-Management Education Program on Medication Adherence and Glycemic Control: A 24-Month Longitudinal Study
proposals.motivation: Pharmacist-led education programs have shown promise in improving short-term diabetes outcomes, but evidence for long-term sustainability is limited. Given the chronic nature of diabetes and the known risk of intervention effect decay, understanding whether initial gains in adherence and glycemic control persist beyond six months is critical for justifying resource allocation and designing effective chronic care models. This addresses a key gap in pharmacy practice and diabetes management literature pre-2026.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that patients who complete a structured pharmacist-led diabetes self-management education program will demonstrate significantly higher medication adherence and better glycemic control (HbA1c) at 12 and 24 months post-intervention compared to a matched usual-care control group, and that these improvements will be sustained from the 6-month assessment.
proposals.method: This will be a prospective, matched-cohort study extending the follow-up of participants from a prior 6-month program evaluation. Using pharmacy claims data, we will calculate medication possession ratio (MPR) and proportion of days covered (PDC) for diabetes medications at 6, 12, 18, and 24 months. HbA1c values will be extracted from electronic health records. We will employ generalized estimating equations (GEE) to model changes in MPR/PDC and HbA1c over time, comparing the intervention cohort to a propensity-score-matched control cohort from the same health system receiving usual care. Subgroup analyses will assess sustainability in populations at higher risk for decline.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust, longitudinal evidence on the durability of pharmacist-led diabetes education, informing clinical guidelines on intervention frequency and booster session needs. It will contribute directly to value-based care metrics by linking a specific pharmacy service to sustained clinical outcomes, strengthening the case for its integration into standard care pathways.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by this researcher and others assessed outcomes at ≤6 months, this proposal uniquely tracks the same cohort for a full two years using objective adherence metrics and clinical data, allowing direct analysis of sustainability and decay patterns rather than extrapolation from short-term results.

proposals.title: Value Engineering for Pharmacist-Led Diabetes Care: A Function-Based Cost-Effectiveness Analysis of a Collaborative Clinic Model
proposals.motivation: Pharmacist-led collaborative care clinics for diabetes management have demonstrated clinical benefits, but their economic sustainability remains a critical barrier to widespread adoption. Traditional cost-effectiveness analyses often treat the clinic as a 'black box,' failing to identify specific, high-cost processes that could be optimized. A systematic, function-based economic evaluation is needed to guide the design of efficient, scalable models that deliver high value in resource-constrained healthcare systems.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a Value Engineering (VE) framework to a pharmacist-led diabetes clinic will identify specific functional processes where cost can be reduced without compromising clinical outcomes, thereby revealing a more favorable and actionable cost-effectiveness profile for the model.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective analysis of an established, long-term (>12 months) pharmacist-led diabetes clinic. Using the VE Job Plan methodology, we will first define the clinic's primary (e.g., achieve glycemic control) and secondary functions (e.g., provide medication education). For each function, we will map associated resource costs (personnel, supplies, space) and link them to quantifiable outcome measures (e.g., change in HbA1c, hospitalizations avoided, patient confidence scores) using data from electronic health records and patient surveys. A function-cost-outcome matrix will be created, and value indices (outcome per unit cost) will be calculated to identify low-value functions for targeted redesign.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, granular blueprint for the cost structure of a collaborative care clinic, moving beyond average cost-per-outcome metrics. It will offer actionable insights for clinic administrators to streamline operations and for policymakers to fund high-value components. The work will establish a replicable VE methodology for evaluating other complex healthcare interventions.
proposals.novelty: This adapts a rigorous industrial engineering framework, Value Engineering, to clinical pharmacy practice evaluation—a significant cross-disciplinary leap. It shifts the analytic focus from the intervention as a whole to its constituent functions, enabling precision optimization not possible with standard pharmacoeconomic models.

proposals.title: A Stepped-Wedge Cluster Randomized Trial of a Pharmacist-Led Diabetes Management Clinic on Long-Term Glycemic Control and Hospitalization Rates in Primary Care
proposals.motivation: Despite established benefits, the integration of clinical pharmacists into primary care for diabetes management remains inconsistent, with a lack of robust, real-world evidence on long-term outcomes. Pre-2026 studies, including those from the All of Us Program, highlight the critical need for sustainable, team-based models to manage chronic conditions and prevent complications like neuropathy. This trial addresses the implementation gap by evaluating a scalable pharmacist-led intervention within diverse primary care settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the sequential implementation of a structured, pharmacist-led diabetes management clinic will result in a sustained reduction in mean HbA1c and a lower rate of diabetes-related hospitalizations compared to usual primary care over a 24-month period.
proposals.method: We will conduct a stepped-wedge cluster randomized trial in 24 primary care practices. Practices will be randomized into 4 sequences (6 clusters each) to cross from the control (usual care) to the intervention phase every 6 months over a total study period of 24 months. The intervention is a standardized pharmacist-led clinic protocol for medication therapy management, education, and monitoring. The primary outcome is the change in mean HbA1c, analyzed using a mixed-effects linear regression model with random intercepts for practice and patient, fixed effects for time period and intervention phase, and adjustment for covariates (age, diabetes duration, baseline HbA1c). Secondary outcomes include hospitalization rates and patient-reported quality of life (using the Audit of Diabetes-Dependent Quality of Life scale).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide high-level evidence for the longitudinal effectiveness of pharmacist integration, directly informing health system implementation. It will yield practical data on the real-world impact on hard clinical endpoints like hospitalizations. The design ensures all participants receive the intervention, addressing ethical concerns while generating rigorous comparative data.
proposals.novelty: The application of the stepped-wedge design, common in public health, to a pharmacist-led chronic care model in primary care is novel, allowing for robust within- and between-practice comparisons over an extended period. It builds upon Dr. Morello's prior work on long-term program impacts by explicitly testing sustainability and clinical application in a randomized, controlled framework.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Impact of Medication Adherence on Cardiovascular Risk Markers in Type 2 Diabetes: A Secondary Analysis of the All of Us Research Program
proposals.motivation: In type 2 diabetes management, medication adherence is critical for glycemic control, but its broader impact on cardiovascular risk markers like blood pressure and lipid levels is less characterized in real-world populations. Cardiovascular disease remains the leading cause of mortality in diabetes, and understanding how adherence influences these modifiable risk factors is essential for comprehensive patient care and pharmacist-led interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that sustained high adherence to guideline-recommended diabetes medications (e.g., metformin, SGLT2 inhibitors, GLP-1 RAs) over a 12-month period is associated with statistically significant improvements in systolic blood pressure and LDL-cholesterol levels, independent of baseline values and concurrent lifestyle changes.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using the All of Us Research Program data (pre-2026 v6 or latest available). Adults with type 2 diabetes prescribed first-line medications will be identified. Adherence will be calculated via Proportion of Days Covered (PDC) over a 12-month measurement period, categorizing patients as adherent (PDC≥80%) or non-adherent. Changes in systolic BP and LDL-C will be compared between groups from baseline to a 12-month follow-up using multivariable linear regression, adjusting for age, sex, BMI, baseline values, and concurrent antihypertensive/lipid-lowering therapy. Sensitivity analyses will explore specific drug classes.
proposals.contribution: This study will quantify the direct association between medication adherence and key cardiovascular risk markers in a large, diverse national cohort, moving beyond HbA1c. It will provide robust, real-world evidence to support pharmacist counseling and adherence programs by demonstrating their potential for holistic cardiovascular risk reduction.
proposals.novelty: While adherence's link to HbA1c is established, this analysis leverages the rich, longitudinal All of Us data to specifically examine its impact on blood pressure and lipids over a sustained period, integrating pharmacy claims with detailed clinical measurements in a way that prior pharmacy practice studies often could not.

proposals.title: Digital Phenotyping of Medication Adherence in Type 2 Diabetes: A Feasibility Study Using Smartphone Sensor Data and the All of Us Research Program
proposals.motivation: Medication non-adherence is a major barrier to effective diabetes management, leading to poor glycemic control and increased complications. Current adherence measures, like self-report or pharmacy refill data, are often subjective, intermittent, or prone to bias. There is a critical need for passive, objective, and continuous methods to monitor adherence behavior in real-world settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that patterns of smartphone usage and sensor data (e.g., screen activity, location, time-of-day routines) can be used to construct a digital phenotype that correlates with and predicts objective measures of medication adherence in patients with type 2 diabetes.
proposals.method: We will recruit a cohort of participants with type 2 diabetes from the All of Us Research Program. Participants will install a custom research app that passively collects anonymized smartphone metadata (screen on/off events, app usage, coarse location via cell tower) and accelerometer data over a 12-month period. Adherence will be measured via electronic pill bottle caps (MEMS) for a reference standard. We will use machine learning models (e.g., Random Forests, Gradient Boosting) to extract features from the digital data (e.g., routine stability, nighttime activity) and compare them to adherence patterns, using time-series analysis to identify pre-adherence behavioral signatures.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal evidence linking passively collected digital phenotyping data to a gold-standard adherence measure in diabetes. It will establish a novel methodological framework for remote adherence monitoring and identify specific digital biomarkers of adherence behavior. The findings could inform the development of low-burden, scalable tools for clinicians and researchers to detect non-adherence early.
proposals.novelty: While digital phenotyping has been explored in mental health, its application to medication adherence in chronic physical disease like diabetes is novel. This approach moves beyond simple reminder apps to infer behavior from ambient data, creating a truly passive and objective metric, which is a significant advance over existing self-reported or pharmacy-based methods.

proposals.title: Co-Designing the Diabetes Tune-Up Clinic: A Human-Centered Approach to Optimize Patient Experience and Clinical Workflow
proposals.motivation: Chronic disease management clinics, like diabetes tune-ups, face challenges with patient engagement, workflow inefficiency, and suboptimal outcomes. Pre-2026, pharmacy practice research has focused on educational programs and long-term medication effects, but less on systematically redesigning the clinical encounter itself using participatory methods. Optimizing this experience is crucial for improving adherence, satisfaction, and long-term health in a complex condition like diabetes.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a structured human-centered design (HCD) process, co-led by patients with diabetes and clinic staff, will generate and implement workflow modifications that significantly improve patient-reported experience measures (PREMs) and reduce perceived visit burden compared to the standard clinic workflow.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-phase HCD methodology. First, in the 'Discover' phase, we will conduct ethnographic observations and semi-structured interviews with patients and staff to map pain points. Second, in the 'Co-Design' phase, we will facilitate a series of workshops using participatory design techniques (e.g., journey mapping, prototyping) to generate new workflow concepts. Finally, in the 'Test' phase, we will implement a pilot workflow and evaluate it using a pre-post study design, comparing PREMs (e.g., PAM-13, PSQ-18) and time-motion metrics collected over a 6-month period to a baseline period.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a replicable framework for applying co-design in ambulatory pharmacy practice, moving beyond educational interventions to structural redesign. It will yield a set of validated, patient-informed clinic workflow prototypes and contribute empirical data on how HCD impacts both patient experience and operational efficiency in chronic care management.
proposals.novelty: While HCD is used in product development and some healthcare settings, its application to optimize the specific workflow of a pharmacist-led diabetes tune-up clinic is novel. This integrates participatory design principles directly into pharmacy practice research, a departure from traditional observational or didactic studies.

proposals.title: Predicting Non-Response to Adherence Interventions in Type 2 Diabetes: A Machine Learning Approach Using Baseline Patient Data from the All of Us Research Program
proposals.motivation: Pharmacist-led adherence interventions are common in diabetes management, but resources are finite. Identifying patients unlikely to improve adherence post-intervention would allow for the reallocation of resources to more responsive patients or the design of alternative, personalized support strategies. This addresses a critical efficiency gap in clinical pharmacy practice.
proposals.hypothesis: Baseline demographic, clinical, and psychosocial data can be used to build a predictive model that accurately identifies patients with type 2 diabetes who will not demonstrate improved medication adherence following a standardized pharmacy education program.
proposals.method: We will use de-identified data from the All of Us Research Program, focusing on participants with type 2 diabetes who have documented participation in a pharmacist-led education program and subsequent adherence measures (e.g., pharmacy refill data). Baseline features will include demographics, comorbidities (e.g., depression), polypharmacy, prior adherence patterns, and social determinants of health. We will train and compare multiple machine learning classifiers, including Random Forest, Gradient Boosting (XGBoost), and regularized logistic regression, using a binary outcome of 'non-improver' vs. 'improver' in adherence over a 6-12 month follow-up period. Model performance will be evaluated using AUC-ROC, precision, and recall.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, data-driven tool for pre-intervention triage in pharmacy practice. It shifts the focus from predicting general non-adherence to predicting non-response to a specific intervention, offering a more actionable insight for clinical decision-making. The findings will inform the development of stratified or adaptive intervention protocols.
proposals.novelty: While many studies predict adherence, this inverts the problem to specifically predict intervention failure, a less explored but pragmatically crucial outcome. It leverages the rich, real-world longitudinal data of the All of Us Program to move beyond simple correlations to predictive modeling for clinical utility.

proposals.title: A Stepped-Care, Minimal-Intervention Maintenance Program to Sustain High Adherence and Glycemic Control in Patients with Type 2 Diabetes: A Pragmatic Randomized Controlled Trial
proposals.motivation: Intensive adherence support programs are resource-intensive and may be unnecessary for patients who already demonstrate good medication-taking behavior. In an era of constrained healthcare resources, a 'one-size-fits-all' approach is inefficient. A tailored, low-intensity maintenance strategy for high adherers could preserve clinical benefits while freeing pharmacist and educator capacity to focus on patients with greater needs.
proposals.hypothesis: Patients with type 2 diabetes and high baseline adherence randomized to a minimal-intervention maintenance program will demonstrate non-inferior rates of long-term medication adherence and glycemic control (HbA1c) over 12 months compared to those receiving standard, higher-intensity care.
proposals.method: We will conduct a pragmatic, parallel-group, non-inferiority randomized controlled trial. Eligible patients from primary care or pharmacy databases with type 2 diabetes, high adherence (PDC ≥ 80% for oral antidiabetics over 6 months), and stable glycemic control (HbA1c < 8.0%) will be randomized 1:1. The intervention arm will receive a 'DUAL' (De-escalated, User-focused, Automated, Low-touch) program: quarterly automated adherence feedback via text/portal, a semi-annual pharmacist-led motivational interviewing check-in, and access to an on-demand educational repository. The control arm will receive standard monthly follow-up per clinic protocol. The primary outcome is the proportion of patients maintaining PDC ≥ 80% at 12 months. Secondary outcomes include change in HbA1c, patient satisfaction, and program cost. Analysis will use an intention-to-treat approach with a pre-specified non-inferiority margin of 10%.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide evidence for the feasibility and effectiveness of a stepped-care model in diabetes management, explicitly de-escalating support for stable patients. It will yield a practical, scalable protocol for health systems and payers to allocate adherence resources more efficiently. The findings will contribute to the literature on long-term sustainability of self-care behaviors initiated by more intensive education programs.
proposals.novelty: While most adherence research focuses on improving poor adherence, this study explicitly targets the understudied population of high adherers to test a de-escalation strategy. It operationalizes a 'minimal effective dose' of support, moving beyond a simple comparison of intervention vs. control to a comparison of two active, but differentially intense, care pathways.

proposals.title: Evaluating a Pharmacist-Led Collaborative Tune-Up Clinic Model for Hypertension Management: A Quasi-Experimental Study
proposals.motivation: Hypertension remains a leading modifiable risk factor for cardiovascular disease, yet control rates are suboptimal, partly due to fragmented care and therapeutic inertia. Pharmacists, as accessible medication experts, are well-positioned to lead structured, collaborative interventions to optimize therapy. This study proposes to adapt a proven pharmacist-led diabetes 'tune-up' clinic model to hypertension management, addressing a critical gap in scalable, team-based care delivery.
proposals.hypothesis: Patients with uncontrolled hypertension enrolled in a pharmacist-led collaborative tune-up clinic will achieve a greater reduction in systolic blood pressure over 6 months, compared to patients receiving usual care. We also hypothesize the intervention will improve medication adherence and patient satisfaction.
proposals.method: We will conduct a quasi-experimental study with a non-equivalent control group design. The intervention is a structured, pharmacist-led hypertension tune-up clinic involving protocol-driven assessment, medication therapy management, and collaborative goal-setting with primary care providers. The primary outcome is the change in systolic blood pressure from baseline to 6 months. We will use a linear mixed-effects model to compare outcomes between groups, adjusting for baseline covariates (e.g., age, comorbidities). Secondary analyses will employ logistic regression for binary outcomes (e.g., goal attainment) and repeated-measures ANOVA for longitudinal trends in patient-reported measures.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide robust evidence for the effectiveness of a structured, transferable pharmacist intervention model for a high-prevalence chronic condition beyond diabetes. It will contribute a detailed protocol for hypertension-focused collaborative care that can be implemented in diverse community or primary care pharmacy settings, potentially informing reimbursement and practice policy.
proposals.novelty: While pharmacist interventions for hypertension exist, this study uniquely applies and tests a formalized 'tune-up clinic' structure—previously validated for diabetes—to hypertension, explicitly evaluating its transferability as a generalizable model for chronic disease management rather than a condition-specific program.

proposals.title: Comparative Effectiveness of Wellness Intervention Formats for Stress Reduction in Student Pharmacists: A Randomized Controlled Trial
proposals.motivation: Chronic stress is a critical, well-documented issue in healthcare education, negatively impacting student well-being and professional development. Prior work, including Dr. Morello's, shows the long-term value of structured wellness programs in pharmacy curricula. However, the optimal delivery format—group sessions, one-on-one coaching, or self-paced digital tools—remains unclear, hindering efficient resource allocation for maximal student benefit.
proposals.hypothesis: Group-based wellness interventions will demonstrate superior effectiveness for stress reduction compared to one-on-one coaching or self-paced digital tools, as measured by validated psychological scales over a sustained follow-up period.
proposals.method: We will conduct a three-arm, parallel-group, randomized controlled trial over a 12-month period. First-year student pharmacists (n=180) will be randomized to: 1) bi-weekly, facilitator-led group sessions; 2) monthly one-on-one coaching; or 3) access to a curated suite of self-paced digital mindfulness and CBT tools. The primary outcome is the change in Perceived Stress Scale (PSS-10) score from baseline to 6 and 12 months. Secondary outcomes include burnout (Maslach Burnout Inventory), self-efficacy, and program adherence. Analysis will use linear mixed-effects models to compare score trajectories between groups, adjusting for baseline covariates.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide direct, comparative evidence to guide the design of wellness initiatives within health professions education. It will identify the most effective intervention modality for sustained stress reduction, allowing educators to implement scalable, evidence-based support programs. Findings will also contribute to the literature on the mechanisms (e.g., social support, accountability) underlying successful wellness interventions.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have evaluated single-format wellness programs, this is a head-to-head comparison of three distinct, commonly used modalities within a single, rigorous trial in pharmacy education. It moves beyond establishing if a program works to determine *how* it should be delivered for optimal impact.

proposals.title: Efficacy of a Tailored Wellness Intervention on Burnout and Resilience in Female Student Pharmacists: A Randomized Controlled Trial
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 literature consistently identifies pharmacy students, particularly women, as a high-risk group for burnout and career-related stress, which can negatively impact academic performance and long-term professional well-being. While general wellness programs exist, there is a lack of evidence-based, targeted interventions designed for the specific stressors faced by female pharmacy students, a gap highlighted in recent work on integrating well-being into pharmacy education.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a wellness education program tailored to the specific stressors reported by female student pharmacists will lead to significantly greater improvements in burnout scores, resilience, and perceived stress over a 12-month period compared to a standard, non-tailored wellness program.
proposals.method: We will conduct a two-arm, parallel-group randomized controlled trial. First, we will use validated surveys (e.g., Maslach Burnout Inventory, Perceived Stress Scale) to identify high-stress female student pharmacists (n=120). Participants will be randomized to receive either: 1) a tailored 8-module digital wellness program (content on imposter syndrome, boundary setting, gendered workplace challenges, mindfulness) or 2) a standard wellness program. Outcomes (burnout, resilience, stress) will be measured at baseline, 3, 6, and 12 months. Data will be analyzed using linear mixed-effects models to compare the longitudinal change between groups, adjusting for covariates.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide one of the first rigorous evaluations of a tailored wellness intervention for a high-risk demographic within healthcare education. It will generate actionable evidence on which specific program components are most effective for improving long-term well-being in female pharmacy students, directly informing curricular and co-curricular support structures.
proposals.novelty: This work moves beyond assessing general wellness program impact to designing and testing content specifically for the psychosocial stressors uniquely reported by female pharmacy students, linking targeted intervention to longitudinal outcomes over a full academic year.

proposals.title: A Comparative Effectiveness Study of Virtual vs. In-Person Delivery of a Pharmacist-Led Wellness Series for Patients with Type 2 Diabetes: Engagement, Knowledge Retention, and Clinical Outcomes
proposals.motivation: The rapid shift to virtual healthcare delivery during the COVID-19 pandemic necessitates rigorous evaluation of its long-term effectiveness compared to traditional in-person models for chronic disease management. For patients with diabetes, sustained engagement in wellness education is critical for self-care and preventing complications, yet it is unclear if virtual formats can achieve comparable or superior engagement and outcomes over a multi-month period. This study addresses a key gap in pharmacy practice and diabetes care by providing evidence to optimize the delivery format of structured wellness programs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a virtual delivery format will achieve equivalent or greater participant engagement (measured by attendance and interaction metrics) and non-inferior improvements in diabetes knowledge, self-efficacy, and hemoglobin A1c levels over a 6-month period compared to an in-person format.
proposals.method: We will conduct a pragmatic, parallel-group, non-inferiority randomized controlled trial. Patient participants with Type 2 Diabetes will be randomized to receive an established 8-session pharmacist-led wellness series either virtually (via a secure, interactive video platform with breakout rooms) or in-person. Engagement will be measured via session attendance, duration of participation, and validated interaction scales. Primary outcomes include change in diabetes knowledge (using the revised Diabetes Knowledge Test) and hemoglobin A1c from baseline to 6 months. Secondary outcomes include self-efficacy (Diabetes Management Self-Efficacy Scale) and participant satisfaction. Data will be analyzed using intention-to-treat analysis, with between-group comparisons made using mixed-effects models for repeated measures to account for the longitudinal design.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust, comparative evidence to guide healthcare systems and pharmacies in allocating resources for chronic disease wellness programs. It will contribute specific metrics on the trade-offs between delivery formats for long-term patient engagement, a crucial factor often missing from short-term evaluations. The findings will directly inform best practices for scalable, effective diabetes education within the evolving landscape of hybrid care models.
proposals.novelty: While many studies have evaluated one-off educational sessions, this proposal uniquely applies a long-term, comparative effectiveness framework—akin to methods used in pharmacoepidemiology (e.g., the All of Us Program)—to a structured wellness *series*, directly testing the sustainability of engagement and outcomes across formats over several months, which is novel for pharmacy practice research.

proposals.title: A Randomized Controlled Trial Evaluating the Long-Term Impact of Acceptance and Commitment Therapy (ACT) Workshops on Psychological Flexibility, Burnout, and Professional Well-Being in Student Pharmacists
proposals.motivation: Pharmacy student burnout and mental distress are well-documented pre-2026, posing risks to both student well-being and future patient care. While wellness initiatives exist, many lack a robust, evidence-based psychological framework. Acceptance and Commitment Therapy (ACT), shown effective for medical resident resilience, offers a promising, theory-driven approach to cultivate psychological flexibility—a core skill for managing academic and clinical stress.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that student pharmacists who participate in a series of ACT-based workshops will demonstrate significantly greater improvements in psychological flexibility and reductions in burnout symptoms at 6- and 12-month follow-ups, compared to a wait-list control group receiving standard wellness resources.
proposals.method: This will be a longitudinal, two-arm randomized controlled trial. A cohort of first-year student pharmacists will be randomized to either the intervention (four 90-minute ACT workshops over two months, adapted from protocols by Kashdan, Hayes, and medical education studies) or a control arm. Validated scales (Acceptance and Action Questionnaire-II, Maslach Burnout Inventory for Students) will be administered at baseline, post-intervention, and at 6 and 12 months. Data will be analyzed using linear mixed-effects models to compare the trajectory of outcomes between groups over the long-term study period, controlling for baseline scores.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal, controlled evidence for ACT as a wellness intervention specifically in pharmacy education. It will contribute a manualized workshop protocol tailored to pharmacy student stressors. Findings will inform whether brief ACT training can produce sustained benefits, potentially offering a scalable model for enhancing professional resilience and preventing burnout in healthcare trainees.
proposals.novelty: While ACT is used in general student wellness, its structured adaptation and long-term evaluation within the specific professional identity and stress context of pharmacy education is novel. This bridges a gap between clinical psychology (ACT) and pharmacy education research, moving beyond generic wellness to target the core psychological processes of resilience.

proposals.title: A Multi-Institutional Consortium Trial to Assess the Generalizability and Moderators of a Pharmacist-Led Wellness and Diabetes Self-Care Education Program
proposals.motivation: Pharmacist-led wellness and chronic disease education programs, like the one previously developed and tested in a single institution, show promise for improving student well-being and clinical knowledge. However, their effectiveness and sustainability across diverse educational settings with varying resources, curricula, and student demographics remain unknown. A multi-institutional study is critical to determine generalizability and identify contextual factors that influence program success, informing scalable implementation strategies for pharmacy education nationwide.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the core wellness and diabetes self-care education program will demonstrate sustained improvements in student knowledge, confidence, and well-being across multiple institutions, but that the magnitude and longevity of these effects will be moderated by institution-specific factors such as curricular integration level, faculty support structures, and baseline student stress levels.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, controlled, multi-site trial across a consortium of 5-7 schools/colleges of pharmacy with varied characteristics. The intervention is the established multi-module program combining wellness principles with diabetes self-care education. We will employ a stepped-wedge cluster design, where institutions sequentially implement the program over 12-18 months. Primary outcomes (knowledge, confidence, well-being metrics) and secondary outcomes (clinical application, burnout) will be measured at baseline, immediately post-program, and at 6- and 12-month follow-ups using validated scales. Institution-level moderators will be captured via structured environmental assessments and analyzed using mixed-effects multivariable regression models.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first robust, multi-institutional evidence for the external validity of an integrated wellness-clinical education program in pharmacy. It will yield a practical framework for implementation, identifying key moderators (e.g., required vs. elective status, peer mentorship models) that optimize outcomes. The findings will directly guide accreditation standards and resource allocation for student well-being initiatives across health professions education.
proposals.novelty: While single-site evaluations exist, this is the first study to systematically test the generalizability of a combined wellness/diabetes education program and to empirically identify institution-level moderators of its long-term impact, moving beyond proof-of-concept to scalable implementation science within pharmacy education.

proposals.title: The Educator's Paradox: A Longitudinal, Mixed-Methods Study of Faculty and Staff Stress in the Context of Developing and Delivering a Student Pharmacist Wellness Program
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 literature extensively documents high rates of burnout and stress among healthcare students and professionals, including pharmacists, leading to calls for wellness interventions. However, the impact of creating and implementing these resource-intensive programs on the faculty and staff responsible for them is largely unstudied. This creates a critical gap, as potential negative impacts on educators could undermine the sustainability and effectiveness of wellness initiatives in pharmacy education.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the process of developing and delivering a student wellness program will be associated with a measurable increase in perceived stress and burnout among participating faculty and staff over a 12-month period, despite potential positive impacts on student outcomes.
proposals.method: This will be a longitudinal, mixed-methods study conducted over 12 months. Faculty and staff involved in a defined student wellness program will complete validated surveys (e.g., Perceived Stress Scale, Maslach Burnout Inventory) at baseline, 6 months, and 12 months. Quantitative scores will be compared between time points using repeated-measures ANOVA. Concurrently, semi-structured interviews will be conducted at each interval to explore qualitative themes related to workload, emotional labor, and role conflict. Thematic analysis will be used to code interview transcripts and integrate findings with quantitative trends.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical evidence of the 'inverse' or secondary impact of wellness programs on educators in a pharmacy context. It will contribute a framework for evaluating the holistic cost-benefit of educational interventions. Findings will inform best practices for institutional support, resource allocation, and program design to protect educator well-being and ensure program sustainability.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Morello et al. measured long-term knowledge retention in students from a diabetes program, this study inverts the focus to the educators' well-being. It explicitly tests the non-obvious premise that efforts to improve student wellness may paradoxically strain the very staff tasked with implementation, a dynamic not captured in existing program evaluations.

proposals.title: Optimizing Pharmacist-Physician Collaborative Care for Diabetes Management: A Lean Healthcare Workflow Intervention Study
proposals.motivation: Pharmacist-physician collaborative care models improve outcomes in chronic disease management like diabetes, but workflow inefficiencies can limit their scalability and sustainability. Pre-2026, Lean methodology has been successfully applied in hospital settings to reduce waste and improve efficiency, yet its systematic application to optimize outpatient collaborative care team workflows, particularly in community or ambulatory care pharmacy settings, remains underexplored. Addressing these inefficiencies is crucial for expanding the reach of these high-impact models.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a structured Lean intervention (e.g., Value Stream Mapping, 5S) within pharmacist-physician collaborative care teams for diabetes management will significantly reduce non-value-added time in patient care workflows and improve process efficiency metrics, without compromising care quality.
proposals.method: This quasi-experimental study will employ a pre-post design comparing workflow metrics over a 12-month period. We will recruit 8-10 established pharmacist-physician collaborative care teams managing type 2 diabetes patients. The technical approach involves a three-phase Lean intervention: 1) Current State Mapping using direct observation and time-motion studies to quantify workflow steps and identify waste (e.g., delays, redundant documentation); 2) a facilitated Kaizen event to design a Future State Map targeting specific inefficiencies; and 3) implementation of the new workflow with Plan-Do-Study-Act cycles. Primary outcomes will be time from referral to comprehensive medication review and proportion of pharmacist time spent on direct patient care activities.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a replicable framework for applying Lean principles to ambulatory collaborative practice, generating practical tools for workflow analysis and redesign. It will contribute empirical evidence on the measurable efficiency gains achievable in pharmacist-physician teams, supporting health system administrators in resource allocation and model expansion for chronic care management.
proposals.novelty: While Lean is established in manufacturing and inpatient care, its tailored application to the specific, communication-intensive workflows of outpatient pharmacist-physician collaborative care is novel. This study moves beyond generic efficiency concepts to provide a disease-specific (diabetes) and team-specific intervention protocol grounded in pre-2026 Lean healthcare techniques.

proposals.title: Evaluating the Transferability of a Pharmacist-Led Diabetes Education Program to Interprofessional Training for Nursing and Medical Students: A Longitudinal Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Interprofessional collaboration is critical for managing chronic diseases like diabetes, yet pre-licensure training often occurs in silos. Pre-2026, pharmacy education has developed robust, longitudinal programs that successfully improve student knowledge, confidence, and clinical application in diabetes care. Leveraging these established pharmacy frameworks to train nursing and medical students presents a scalable, efficient model to build foundational interprofessional competencies early in health professions education.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adapting and implementing a validated, pharmacist-led diabetes self-care education program as an interprofessional training environment for nursing and medical students will lead to sustained, non-inferior improvements in diabetes-specific knowledge, interprofessional confidence, and clinical application skills compared to the original pharmacy-only cohort over a 12-month period.
proposals.method: This will be a longitudinal, quasi-experimental study comparing three cohorts: 1) the original pharmacy student cohort, 2) a new cohort of nursing students, and 3) a new cohort of medical students. The core intervention is the adapted program, delivered over one academic term. Outcomes (knowledge via validated exams, confidence via interprofessional attitudes scales, and clinical application via standardized patient assessments) will be measured at baseline, immediately post-program, and at 6 and 12 months. Analysis will employ mixed-effects models to compare trends between groups over time, adjusting for baseline scores and demographic covariates.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide empirical evidence on the transferability and effectiveness of a pharmacy-originated educational model for building diabetes care competencies in other health professions. It will contribute a practical, tested framework for accelerating interprofessional education (IPE) integration. Findings will inform curriculum design by identifying core, transferable program elements versus profession-specific adaptations needed for optimal outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While interprofessional training is not new, this proposal innovatively uses a mature, outcomes-validated pharmacy program as the foundational *training environment* for other professions, moving beyond ad-hoc IPE sessions to a structured, disease-specific model with proven long-term impact. It tests the concept of 'educational model transfer' rather than simple collaboration.

proposals.title: The Adaptive Stress Curve: A Longitudinal Study of Stress, Academic Performance, and Well-Being in Student Pharmacists
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 pharmacy education research, including Dr. Morello's work on student well-being and diabetes education programs, often frames stress as a uniformly negative factor to be mitigated. However, psychological theories like the Yerkes-Dodson law suggest a non-linear relationship where moderate arousal can enhance performance. This study directly challenges the monolithic 'stress is bad' paradigm within pharmacy education, seeking to identify if and for whom stress can be adaptive, informing more nuanced student support strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a moderate level of perceived academic stress will be associated with higher academic performance in student pharmacists compared to both low and high stress levels, and that this relationship will be moderated by individual factors such as resilience and coping style.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, longitudinal cohort study over 12 months, tracking first- and second-year student pharmacists. Perceived stress will be measured bimonthly using the Perceived Stress Scale (PSS-10). Academic performance will be measured via semester GPA. We will employ linear mixed-effects models to analyze the repeated measures, testing for a quadratic (inverted-U) relationship between stress and GPA. Moderation analysis will be performed by including interaction terms between stress scores and baseline measures of resilience (Connor-Davidson Resilience Scale) and coping (Brief COPE inventory).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide empirical evidence for a more nuanced understanding of stress in health professions education. It will contribute a data-driven framework for identifying students who may benefit from stress-reduction interventions versus those who might thrive under moderate challenge. The findings will directly inform the design of targeted academic and well-being programs within pharmacy curricula.
proposals.novelty: While prior work in this domain, including Dr. Morello's, focuses on reducing negative outcomes, this study uniquely applies a classic psychological model to test for potential benefits of stress in a high-stakes academic environment. It moves beyond assessing 'long-term impact' of programs to dissect the dynamic, non-linear role of stress itself over time.

proposals.title: Identifying the Resilient Student Pharmacist: A Longitudinal, Mixed-Methods Study of Stress, Coping, and Academic Success in a Demanding Curriculum
proposals.motivation: Pharmacy education is notoriously stressful, with high rates of burnout and mental health challenges documented pre-2026. While most research focuses on identifying and mitigating student distress, the inverse problem—systematically identifying students who thrive—is understudied. Understanding the protective factors and adaptive coping strategies of resilient students can inform the design of targeted, strength-based wellness programs to support the entire cohort.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a distinct, measurable profile of resilient student pharmacists exists, characterized by specific combinations of personal traits, coping behaviors, and social support systems that predict sustained low stress and high academic performance over the long term, despite curricular demands.
proposals.method: We propose a longitudinal, observational cohort study over 24 months, tracking first-year student pharmacists through their core curriculum. Quantitative data (bi-annual validated surveys on perceived stress, coping inventory, resilience scale, social support, and academic performance) will be analyzed using latent profile analysis and mixed-effects models to identify resilient subgroups. Concurrently, purposive sampling of resilient-identified students will undergo in-depth qualitative interviews to explore lived experiences and coping mechanisms. Data integration will follow a convergent mixed-methods design.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a validated, multi-dimensional profile of the resilient pharmacy student, moving beyond deficit models. It will contribute a framework for proactive student support by identifying modifiable protective factors that can be cultivated through curricular and co-curricular programming, ultimately aiming to improve student well-being and professional development.
proposals.novelty: This work inverts the traditional problem frame from 'who is at risk' to 'who is resilient and why,' applying a positive psychology lens to pharmacy education. It integrates long-term quantitative tracking with rich qualitative exploration specifically to model resilience, a approach distinct from prior cross-sectional studies of stress prevalence.

proposals.title: Discrepancy in Perception: A Mixed-Methods Study of Pharmacy Faculty Assessments of Student Stress and Its Correlation with Academic Performance
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 literature consistently identifies high stress and burnout among pharmacy students, negatively impacting well-being and learning. Faculty are central to the educational environment, yet it is unknown if their perceptions of student stress align with student self-reports. This gap is critical because misaligned perceptions could hinder timely support and the effectiveness of wellness-integrated curricula, a key theme in Morello's prior work.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant discrepancy exists between faculty perceptions of student stress levels and students' self-reported stress, and that this perceptual gap is associated with lower student academic performance in clinically intensive courses.
proposals.method: We will employ a convergent parallel mixed-methods design. Quantitatively, we will administer validated surveys (e.g., Perceived Stress Scale) to a cohort of student pharmacists and a parallel perception survey to their faculty at two time points (mid-semester and finals). Academic performance will be measured via course grades. Discrepancy scores will be calculated and analyzed using linear regression, controlling for covariates. Qualitatively, we will conduct semi-structured interviews with a purposive sample of faculty to explore the foundations of their perceptions.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical evidence quantifying the faculty-student perception gap regarding stress in pharmacy education. It will offer actionable insights for refining faculty development programs and tailoring student support interventions, directly extending Morello's research on long-term educational program impact and holistic pharmacy education.
proposals.novelty: While student stress is well-documented, systematically measuring and comparing it against faculty perception to link this discrepancy to concrete academic outcomes is novel. This moves beyond describing stress to analyzing a key relational dynamic in the educational environment that may perpetuate or mitigate it.

proposals.title: Comparative Effectiveness of Pharmacist-Led vs. Nurse-Led vs. Peer Educator-Led Diabetes Self-Management Education on Sustained Glycemic Control and Patient-Reported Outcomes: A Pragmatic, Cluster-Randomized Trial
proposals.motivation: Pharmacist-led education programs have demonstrated efficacy in improving diabetes outcomes, but healthcare systems face workforce constraints and seek cost-effective models. It is unclear if the unique medication expertise and longitudinal patient relationships of pharmacists are essential drivers of success, or if similar outcomes can be achieved by other providers. Determining the comparative effectiveness of different educators is critical for optimizing resource allocation in chronic care management.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pharmacist-led diabetes self-management education will result in superior long-term glycemic control (HbA1c reduction) and patient self-efficacy compared to programs led by registered nurses or trained peer educators, due to pharmacists' specialized pharmacotherapy knowledge and accessibility.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 24-month, three-arm, pragmatic cluster-randomized trial within a large integrated health network. Primary care clinics will be randomized to have their eligible patients with uncontrolled type 2 diabetes receive a standardized 6-month education program delivered by either (1) clinical pharmacists, (2) diabetes nurse educators, or (3) trained peer educators. The primary outcome is the change in HbA1c from baseline to 12 and 24 months. Secondary outcomes include diabetes self-efficacy (DES-SF), medication adherence (via pharmacy refill data), and healthcare utilization. We will use linear mixed-effects models to compare outcomes between arms, adjusting for baseline covariates and clustering by clinic.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide direct, high-quality evidence on the value-added role of pharmacists versus other educators in diabetes care. It will inform health system administrators and policymakers on how to structure team-based diabetes education programs effectively. The findings will contribute to the literature on sustainable care delivery models for chronic diseases.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies compare pharmacist intervention to usual care, this proposal directly tests the *agent* of delivery, isolating the 'pharmacist effect' against other plausible educators using a standardized curriculum. The long-term (24-month) follow-up addresses a key gap in understanding the sustainability of knowledge and behavior change initiated by different providers.

proposals.title: Evaluating a Pharmacist-Led Protocolized Management Program as a Substitute for Routine Physician Visits in Stable Type 2 Diabetes: A Non-Inferiority Randomized Controlled Trial
proposals.motivation: The growing prevalence of type 2 diabetes strains primary care capacity, leading to long wait times and reduced physician availability for complex cases. Pharmacists are increasingly integrated into chronic care models, but their role is typically framed as complementary. Testing a structured, protocol-driven pharmacist program as a potential substitute for specific, routine physician visits could enhance healthcare system efficiency without compromising patient safety, addressing a critical pre-2026 access challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: For adults with stable, uncomplicated type 2 diabetes, a pharmacist-led protocolized management program substituting for scheduled quarterly physician follow-up visits will be non-inferior to standard physician-led care in maintaining glycemic control (HbA1c) and reducing diabetes-related complications over 12 months.
proposals.method: We will conduct a pragmatic, parallel-group, randomized controlled non-inferiority trial. Patients with stable T2D (HbA1c 6.5-8.5% for ≥6 months) will be randomized 1:1 to intervention (protocolized pharmacist visits replacing 2 of 4 quarterly physician visits) or control (standard physician-led care). The primary outcome is the change in HbA1c at 12 months, with a pre-specified non-inferiority margin of 0.4%. Secondary outcomes include blood pressure, lipid levels, diabetes distress scale scores, healthcare utilization, and cost-effectiveness. Data will be analyzed using a mixed-effects model for repeated measures with an intention-to-treat approach.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust, prospective evidence on the safety and efficacy of a delegated care model for stable chronic disease management. It will contribute a replicable protocol for pharmacist-led substitution and generate data on patient-reported outcomes and economic impact, informing health system policy and clinical guideline development for team-based care.
proposals.novelty: While pharmacist integration is well-studied, this proposal directly tests substitution rather than addition, a conceptually distinct and systemically impactful model. It moves beyond assessing knowledge or adherence to evaluating hard clinical endpoints and non-inferiority in a real-world, long-term comparative effectiveness framework.

proposals.title: Evaluating a Patient-Centered, Behavior-First Intervention for Type 2 Diabetes: A Randomized Controlled Trial Comparing Empowerment Metrics to Short-Term A1C Change
proposals.motivation: Current diabetes management often prioritizes rapid A1C reduction, which can overshadow patient-centered goals like self-efficacy and sustainable behavior change. This focus may lead to therapeutic inertia or patient disengagement if short-term glycemic targets are not met. A paradigm shift towards empowering patients and building foundational self-care skills could improve long-term outcomes and quality of life, a core tenet of pharmacy practice.
proposals.hypothesis: A pharmacist-led intervention prioritizing patient empowerment and behavior change metrics over a 3-month period will lead to greater improvements in diabetes self-care activities and patient-reported outcomes at 6 months, without inferior long-term (12-month) A1C control, compared to an intervention focused primarily on achieving short-term A1C reduction.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 12-month, two-arm, randomized controlled trial.
