Patient participants (n=200) with suboptimally controlled Type 2 diabetes will be randomized to either a 'Behavior-First' arm (primary outcomes: change in Diabetes Empowerment Scale score and self-care inventory at 3 months) or an 'A1C-First' arm (primary outcome: A1C change at 3 months). Both arms receive standardized pharmacist counseling, but the intervention focus and goal-setting differ. Outcomes will be compared between groups at 3, 6, and 12 months using mixed-effects models, with the primary analysis testing for non-inferiority in 12-month A1C between arms.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide empirical evidence for a patient-empowerment model in diabetes management, directly informing clinical pharmacy practice. It will generate a validated framework for measuring success in diabetes interventions beyond glycemic metrics alone, contributing to more holistic patient care standards.
proposals.novelty: While behavior change is recognized as important, this study explicitly tests a clinical protocol that de-prioritizes short-term A1C as the primary goal, challenging a deeply entrenched clinical norm. It operationalizes and tests the 'patient empowerment' paradigm in a head-to-head comparison against standard target-driven care.

proposals.title: Impact of a Pharmacy-Led, System-Level Metformin Monitoring Protocol on Long-Term Neuropathy Risk in Patients with Type 2 Diabetes: A Quasi-Experimental Study
proposals.motivation: Long-term metformin use is associated with vitamin B12 deficiency and neuropathy, yet routine monitoring is inconsistent, leaving patients at risk. Active patient enrollment in education or monitoring programs faces barriers like attrition and resource intensity. System-level interventions that operate passively within the pharmacy workflow could improve outcomes without relying on sustained patient participation, addressing a critical gap in chronic disease management.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a standardized, pharmacy-led protocol for annual vitamin B12 screening for all patients dispensed metformin for ≥12 months will increase screening rates and reduce the incidence of documented neuropathy over a 24-month period compared to usual care.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 24-month, multi-site, quasi-experimental study comparing clinics with and without the protocol. The intervention arm will implement an EHR-embedded clinical decision support tool that automatically flags eligible patients (metformin prescription ≥12 months, no B12 test in prior year) during prescription verification at the pharmacy. Pharmacists will then order the test per protocol. We will use interrupted time series analysis to compare screening rates and logistic regression to compare neuropathy incidence (ICD-10 codes) between groups, adjusting for age, diabetes duration, and metformin dose.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide evidence for the effectiveness of a scalable, pharmacy-driven, system-level intervention to mitigate a known adverse drug event. It will demonstrate how integrating proactive monitoring into the dispensing workflow can improve long-term outcomes without requiring active patient enrollment in a separate program.
proposals.novelty: While Morello's prior work assessed outcomes of active patient education programs, this study innovates by testing a fully passive, pharmacy-operational intervention. It leverages the pharmacist's role in medication dispensing to systematically enact a monitoring guideline, a shift from patient-dependent to system-dependent care improvement.

proposals.title: Sustained Glycemic Control After a Pharmacist-Led Education Program: A 24-Month Longitudinal Follow-up Study in Patients with Type 2 Diabetes
proposals.motivation: Pharmacist-led diabetes self-management education programs have demonstrated short-term efficacy in improving glycemic control, typically measured at 3-6 months post-intervention. However, the long-term durability of these improvements is poorly characterized, limiting understanding of their true clinical and economic value. Assessing whether initial A1C reductions are sustained beyond six months is critical for justifying the integration of such programs into standard, long-term chronic care models.
proposals.hypothesis: Patients with type 2 diabetes who participated in a structured, pharmacist-led self-care education program will maintain statistically significant improvements in A1C levels at 12, 18, and 24 months post-intervention compared to their baseline, whereas a matched control group will not show sustained improvement.
proposals.method: This will be a longitudinal, quasi-experimental cohort study with a matched historical control group. Participants from the original 6-month study will be followed for an additional 18 months. The primary outcome is change in A1C from baseline to 12, 18, and 24 months. Data will be extracted from electronic health records. We will use generalized estimating equations (GEE) with an autoregressive correlation structure to model the longitudinal A1C data, adjusting for baseline covariates (age, diabetes duration, medication regimen). The control group will be constructed via propensity score matching (nearest neighbor, caliper 0.2) from a pool of eligible patients who did not receive the intervention during the same period.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust evidence on the long-term durability of pharmacist-led diabetes education, moving beyond typical short-term assessments. It will inform healthcare systems and payers about the potential for sustained clinical benefits, supporting the argument for recurring or booster intervention models. The findings will directly contribute to the design of more effective, long-term chronic disease management programs within pharmacy practice.
proposals.novelty: While short-term studies are common, a rigorous 24-month follow-up specifically of a pharmacist-delivered program in a real-world setting is novel. The use of advanced longitudinal modeling (GEE) combined with propensity score matching for long-term comparator data extends the methodological rigor typically applied in pharmacy practice research.

proposals.title: Cost-Effectiveness Analysis of a Pharmacist-Led Diabetes Self-Care Education Program Compared to Usual Physician Care: A Pragmatic Trial
proposals.motivation: Diabetes imposes a substantial economic burden on healthcare systems, with costs driven by complications and hospitalizations. While pharmacist-led interventions show clinical promise, their economic value compared to standard physician care remains inadequately quantified, hindering broader implementation and reimbursement decisions. A rigorous cost-effectiveness analysis is needed to inform healthcare policy and resource allocation for diabetes management.
proposals.hypothesis: A structured, pharmacist-led diabetes self-care education program will be cost-effective compared to usual physician care over a 12-month period, demonstrating a favorable incremental cost-effectiveness ratio (ICER) by reducing diabetes-related complications and improving quality-adjusted life years (QALYs).
proposals.method: We will conduct a cost-utility analysis alongside a 12-month, two-arm pragmatic trial. Patients with type 2 diabetes will be randomized to either the pharmacist-led program (based on Morello's prior educational model) or usual physician care. We will adopt a healthcare payer perspective, collecting direct medical costs (program delivery, medications, outpatient visits, hospitalizations) and measuring effectiveness via QALYs derived from the EQ-5D-5L. The primary analysis will calculate the ICER, with deterministic and probabilistic sensitivity analyses (using Monte Carlo simulation with 10,000 iterations) to assess uncertainty.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a robust economic evaluation of a specific pharmacist-led intervention, offering actionable evidence for healthcare administrators and payers. It will extend the evidence base beyond clinical outcomes to include economic value, supporting the business case for integrating pharmacists into chronic care teams.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Morello et al. established the long-term knowledge and clinical impact of the education program, this study uniquely integrates those clinical outcomes with a formal, trial-based economic evaluation to directly compare its cost-effectiveness against the physician-care standard.

proposals.title: Predictors of Glycemic Response: A Subgroup Analysis of Patient Demographics and Baseline Characteristics in a Pharmacist-Led Diabetes Self-Care Education Program
proposals.motivation: Pharmacist-led diabetes self-management education (DSME) programs are effective but show variable patient outcomes. In the pre-2026 era of personalized medicine, identifying which patients derive maximal glycemic benefit is crucial for optimizing resource allocation and tailoring interventions. This analysis addresses a key gap in understanding the real-world effectiveness of established pharmacy education programs for specific patient subgroups.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific baseline patient characteristics (e.g., age, duration of diabetes, initial A1C, medication regimen complexity, or prior DSME exposure) will significantly predict the magnitude of A1C reduction following participation in the sustained pharmacist-led education program.
proposals.method: We will conduct a secondary analysis of longitudinal data from the previously implemented Student Pharmacist Diabetes Self-Care Education Program. Using pre- and post-program A1C values as the primary outcome, we will employ multivariable linear regression to identify independent predictors of A1C change. To identify discrete subgroups with maximal benefit, we will perform a Classification and Regression Tree (CART) analysis, a pre-2026 machine learning method suitable for clinical subgroup identification, using key demographic and clinical baseline variables as splitters.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide actionable evidence for pharmacists to prospectively identify patients most likely to achieve significant glycemic improvement through DSME, enhancing program efficiency. It will contribute a methodology for evaluating heterogeneity of treatment effect within pharmacy practice-based research, moving beyond average program effects to personalized impact.
proposals.novelty: While prior work established the program's long-term average efficacy, applying CART analysis to identify interpretable patient subgroups for maximal glycemic benefit within a pharmacy education context is novel. This moves beyond simple associations to define practical, data-driven eligibility or prioritization criteria.

proposals.title: Long-Term Impact of a Pharmacist-Led Diabetes Self-Care Education Program on Clinical Outcomes and Patient-Reported Measures: A 12-Month Follow-Up Study
proposals.motivation: Pharmacist-led education programs have shown promise in improving diabetes knowledge and self-care, but their long-term impact on hard clinical endpoints and patient-reported outcomes remains understudied. Given the high burden of cardiovascular complications and poor quality of life in patients with diabetes, it is critical to determine if educational interventions yield sustained improvements in blood pressure, lipid control, medication adherence, and overall well-being, beyond immediate knowledge gains.
proposals.hypothesis: Patients with type 2 diabetes who complete a structured, pharmacist-led self-care education program will demonstrate significantly greater improvements in systolic blood pressure, LDL cholesterol, medication adherence, and diabetes-specific quality of life scores over a 12-month period compared to a matched usual-care control group.
proposals.method: This will be a prospective, quasi-experimental cohort study with a 12-month follow-up period. Participants will be recruited from community pharmacy and primary care settings and assigned to the intervention (a multi-session diabetes education program delivered by pharmacists) or a usual-care control group. Clinical outcomes (blood pressure, HbA1c, lipid panel) will be measured at baseline, 6, and 12 months. Patient-reported outcomes will be assessed via validated surveys (e.g., Morisky Medication Adherence Scale-8, Audit of Diabetes-Dependent Quality of Life) at the same intervals. Data analysis will employ linear mixed-effects models to compare the trajectory of change between groups over time, adjusting for baseline characteristics.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust, longitudinal evidence on the holistic clinical value of pharmacist-led diabetes education, moving beyond knowledge assessment to tangible health metrics. It will inform healthcare payers and policymakers about the potential of such programs to improve cardiovascular risk factors and patient-centered outcomes, thereby strengthening the case for their integration into standard care pathways.
proposals.novelty: While previous work by this team and others has focused on knowledge and confidence, this study uniquely employs a long-term, multi-domain outcome framework to capture the program's sustained effect on both objective clinical parameters and subjective patient experience, filling a critical evidence gap.

proposals.title: Gamification of a Pharmacist-Led Diabetes Self-Management Program: A Randomized Controlled Trial Assessing Long-Term Adherence and Glycemic Control
proposals.motivation: Long-term adherence to diabetes self-care behaviors (e.g., glucose monitoring, medication management, physical activity) remains a critical challenge, directly impacting glycemic control and complication risk. Pre-2026 evidence from fitness and education apps demonstrates that gamification (points, badges, leaderboards) can enhance user engagement and habit formation. Applying these principles within a structured, pharmacist-led program could address the engagement gap in chronic disease management.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a gamified diabetes self-management program, compared to a standard non-gamified version, will lead to significantly greater patient adherence to self-care tasks and improved long-term glycemic control (HbA1c) over a 12-month period.
proposals.method: We will conduct a two-arm, parallel-group randomized controlled trial. Patients with type 2 diabetes (n=200) will be randomized to either a standard pharmacist-led education program or a gamified version. The gamified intervention will be delivered via a custom mobile app incorporating established mechanics: (1) a points and leveling system for completing self-care logs and educational modules, (2) achievement badges for streaks and goal attainment, and (3) a cooperative team challenge component. Primary outcomes, measured at baseline, 6, and 12 months, will be adherence scores (via validated self-report and app-logged data) and HbA1c levels. Analysis will use linear mixed-effects models to compare changes between groups over time.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust, longitudinal evidence on the efficacy of gamification in a clinical pharmacy context, moving beyond short-term feasibility studies. It will contribute a validated, scalable intervention framework that pharmacists can integrate into patient care services to improve sustained self-management. Findings will also elucidate which behavioral mechanisms (e.g., social relatedness, mastery) are most activated by the gamified elements.
proposals.novelty: While gamification is common in consumer apps, its rigorous integration and evaluation within a longitudinal, pharmacist-supervised chronic disease management program, with direct clinical outcome measures, is novel. This bridges the gap between digital engagement tools and evidence-based clinical pharmacy practice.

proposals.title: Adaptive Learning for Personalized Diabetes Self-Management Education: A Randomized Controlled Trial of an Intelligent Tutoring System for Patient Pharmacist Programs
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Morello's own work, shows that structured diabetes education programs improve knowledge and confidence, but their long-term impact is limited by a 'one-size-fits-all' delivery. Intelligent Tutoring Systems (ITS), which adapt educational content based on learner performance, have shown success in academic settings but have not been rigorously applied to personalize chronic disease self-management education for patients in a pharmacy context.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a pharmacist-led diabetes self-management education program enhanced by an adaptive learning ITS algorithm will lead to greater and more sustained improvements in patient clinical outcomes (e.g., HbA1c), self-efficacy, and medication adherence over a 12-month period compared to a standard, non-adaptive education program.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 12-month, two-arm randomized controlled trial. Patients with type 2 diabetes will be enrolled in a pharmacist-led education program. The intervention arm will use a custom ITS platform employing a Bayesian Knowledge Tracing (BKT) algorithm to model each patient's mastery of educational modules (e.g., medication use, glucose monitoring, nutrition). The system will dynamically adjust the sequence, pacing, and review content based on individual quiz performance and interaction data. The control arm will receive the same core content in a fixed, linear sequence. Outcomes (HbA1c, validated survey scores, pharmacy refill data) will be measured at baseline, 6, and 12 months and compared between groups using mixed-effects models.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical evidence for the efficacy of an ITS-adapted patient education program in a community pharmacy or ambulatory care setting. It will contribute a scalable digital framework for personalizing chronic disease management support and generate novel data on the relationship between educational engagement patterns and long-term clinical outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While ITS are established in formal education, their application to adapt real-world, pharmacist-delivered patient counseling for a chronic condition like diabetes is novel. This moves beyond static online modules by creating a closed-loop system where patient performance directly and algorithmically shapes the educational intervention in real time.

proposals.title: A Randomized Controlled Trial Comparing a Community Health Worker-Enhanced Diabetes Self-Care Program to Standard Pharmacy Education for Long-Term Glycemic Control
proposals.motivation: Despite effective pharmacy-led diabetes education programs, long-term maintenance of self-care behaviors and glycemic control remains a challenge, particularly in underserved communities. Community Health Worker (CHW) models from public health have shown success in improving health outcomes by bridging clinical care and community settings, but their structured integration with pharmacist-led education for sustained diabetes management is understudied.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a diabetes self-care education program delivered by student pharmacists and reinforced by trained CHWs in community settings will lead to superior long-term improvements in patient knowledge, self-care behaviors, and HbA1c levels compared to the standard pharmacy-led program alone.
proposals.method: This will be a two-arm, parallel-group, randomized controlled trial conducted over 18 months. Patients with type 2 diabetes (n=200) will be randomized to either a standard student pharmacist-led education program (control) or the same program plus 12 months of structured reinforcement by trained CHWs (intervention). The primary outcome is the change in HbA1c from baseline to 12 and 18 months. Secondary outcomes include changes in diabetes knowledge, self-efficacy, and self-care activities scores, measured at 0, 6, 12, and 18 months. Analysis will use linear mixed-effects models to compare outcomes between groups over time, adjusting for baseline characteristics.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust evidence on the long-term effectiveness of integrating CHWs into pharmacy practice for chronic disease management. It will yield a scalable model for sustaining the benefits of clinical education through community-based support, directly addressing disparities in diabetes care. The findings will inform interprofessional practice and reimbursement models for team-based care.
proposals.novelty: While CHWs and pharmacists have operated in parallel, this proposal uniquely tests a formalized, longitudinal partnership where CHWs are trained to specifically reinforce a standardized pharmacy curriculum, creating a continuous care loop from the academic clinic to the patient's home environment.

proposals.title: Predicting Non-Response to a Pharmacist-Led Diabetes Self-Care Education Program: A Machine Learning Approach to Identify Patients with Minimal A1C Improvement
proposals.motivation: Pharmacist-led diabetes self-management education (DSME) programs are effective for many patients, but resources are finite and not all patients benefit equally. Identifying patients unlikely to achieve meaningful A1C reduction before program enrollment would allow for targeted resource allocation and the development of alternative, personalized interventions. This addresses a critical gap in value-based care by moving from a one-size-fits-all model to a precision public health approach in pharmacy practice.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a machine learning model, trained on pre-program demographic, clinical, and psychosocial data, can accurately identify patients who will show no clinically significant improvement in A1C (defined as a reduction of <0.5%) following completion of a structured DSME program.
proposals.method: We will use data from a longitudinal cohort of patients who completed a previously studied pharmacist-led DSME program. Features will include baseline A1C, diabetes duration, medication regimen (including long-term metformin use), comorbidities, demographics, and pre-program survey data on knowledge and confidence. We will train and compare multiple algorithms, including logistic regression, random forests, and gradient boosting machines (XGBoost), using a 70/30 train-test split. Model performance will be evaluated using AUC-ROC, precision-recall curves, and feature importance analysis to identify key predictors of non-response.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, data-driven tool for pre-program patient triage, enhancing the efficiency of clinical pharmacy services. It will also elucidate modifiable and non-modifiable factors associated with program non-response, informing the design of future adaptive interventions. The study will contribute a novel 'inverse outcome' predictive framework to the pharmacy practice research literature.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Morello et al. established the long-term positive impact of DSME programs, this study flips the analytical focus to predict non-responders—a less studied but pragmatically crucial outcome. It integrates clinical pharmacy data with modern machine learning techniques specifically to address resource optimization, a novel application within this domain.

proposals.title: Impact of a Workflow-Embedded Diabetes Self-Care Education Protocol on Long-Term Patient Outcomes and Pharmacy Efficiency: A Pragmatic Cluster-Randomized Trial
proposals.motivation: Diabetes self-management education (DSME) is critical for improving glycemic control and preventing complications, but access remains limited. Community pharmacies are highly accessible yet underutilized for structured DSME delivery due to perceived workflow disruptions. Integrating proven educational elements into routine medication therapy management (MTM) and dispensing consultations could sustainably scale DSME access, leveraging pharmacists' established patient relationships.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a structured, workflow-embedded DSME protocol, delivered during routine pharmacy encounters, will lead to superior long-term improvements in patient HbA1c and diabetes self-efficacy compared to standard care, without negatively impacting pharmacy operational metrics.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 12-month, cluster-randomized pragmatic trial in 30 community pharmacies. Pharmacies will be randomized to implement the embedded protocol (intervention) or continue usual care (control). The intervention protocol integrates brief, structured DSME elements (e.g., medication adherence checks, foot care reminders, healthy eating prompts) into MTM and dispensing software using a standardized template. Primary outcomes are change in patient HbA1c (measured at baseline, 6, and 12 months) and pharmacy workflow efficiency (time per consultation, total daily patient volume). Data will be analyzed using mixed-effects models, comparing outcomes between groups over time, adjusting for clustering.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust evidence on the effectiveness and feasibility of integrating DSME into standard pharmacy practice. It will yield a replicable protocol for workflow integration that balances clinical impact with operational practicality, directly informing scalable implementation strategies for community pharmacy-based chronic disease management.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have tested educational programs in pharmacy, this work uniquely evaluates the systematic embedding of DSME into existing workflow touchpoints (MTM, dispensing) as a sustainable service model, assessing both clinical outcomes and operational impact—a dual focus rarely combined in prior research.

proposals.title: Assessing and Comparing Knowledge Gaps and Risk Perceptions Regarding Common Medications Among Student Pharmacists and Other Health Science Students: A Cross-Sectional Study
proposals.motivation: Health science students are future healthcare providers, and their knowledge and perceptions directly impact patient safety and public health. Pre-2026 literature, including Morello's work on student pharmacist diabetes education and metformin-related risks, highlights that even trained students can have significant knowledge gaps. It is critical to empirically test the assumption that these students are uniformly well-informed, especially regarding widely used medications with known risks like NSAIDs, antibiotics, or metformin, where misperceptions could lead to poor future clinical decisions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that student pharmacists will demonstrate more accurate knowledge of medication risks compared to other health science students (e.g., nursing, pre-med), but that significant knowledge gaps and risk misperceptions will exist across all groups, particularly for over-the-counter medications and long-term therapy risks.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-institution, cross-sectional survey of health science students (pharmacy, nursing, medicine, physician assistant). The instrument will assess objective knowledge (true/false, multiple-choice) and perceived risk (Likert scales) for a curated list of medications (e.g., metformin/B12, NSAIDs/renal, opioids). Data analysis will employ multivariate logistic regression to identify predictors of knowledge gaps, controlling for program type, year of study, and prior experience. Subgroup comparisons between cohorts will be made using ANCOVA, and correlations between knowledge scores and risk perceptions will be analyzed.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a benchmark of medication risk literacy among future healthcare professionals, identifying specific, high-priority curricular gaps. It will contribute evidence-based insights for tailoring interprofessional education modules focused on common medication risks and inform the development of targeted educational interventions within health science programs to mitigate these knowledge deficits before students enter practice.
proposals.novelty: While prior work often focuses on a single student cohort or disease state (e.g., diabetes), this study directly compares multiple health science disciplines on a common set of medication risks, challenging the blanket assumption of superior preparedness. It links objective knowledge to subjective risk perception, a nuanced approach not commonly taken in pre-2026 assessments of student pharmacist knowledge.

proposals.title: Biochemical Verification of Smoking Status in Student Pharmacist Health Studies: A Comparative Analysis of Self-Report, Cotinine, and Carbon Monoxide Testing
proposals.motivation: Accurate assessment of smoking status is critical for health research and intervention studies, particularly in student populations where social desirability bias may lead to under-reporting. Prior work by Morello et al. on long-term educational programs for diabetes and metformin use in the All of Us Program relies on self-reported data, which may be subject to misclassification. Validating self-report with objective measures is a necessary methodological step to ensure the integrity of findings in pharmacy education and patient care research.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that supplementing self-reported smoking status with biochemical verification (cotinine and CO testing) will reveal a significant rate of misclassification among student pharmacists, and that the agreement between self-report and biochemical measures will be influenced by factors such as program year and participation in health-focused curricula.
proposals.method: We will conduct a cross-sectional study of student pharmacists across multiple years of training. Participants will complete a confidential survey including self-reported smoking frequency and will then provide a saliva sample for cotinine analysis (using a quantitative immunoassay kit) and an exhaled breath sample for carbon monoxide (CO) testing (using a Bedfont Smokerlyzer). A pre-defined cotinine cut-off (e.g., >10 ng/mL) and CO cut-off (e.g., >6 ppm) will define biochemical positivity. We will calculate the prevalence of smoking by each method, determine the sensitivity and specificity of self-report, and use multivariable logistic regression to analyze factors associated with discordance between self-report and biochemical verification.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a validated methodological framework for incorporating biochemical verification into health behavior research within pharmacy education. It will yield concrete data on the reliability of self-reported smoking in this cohort, strengthening the evidence base for future longitudinal studies on student well-being and clinical outcomes. The findings will directly inform the design of more robust assessments in programs like the diabetes self-care education program previously studied.
proposals.novelty: While biochemical verification is standard in smoking cessation trials, its application as a routine validation tool in studies of pharmacy student health behaviors and educational program outcomes is novel. This work bridges the gap between clinical toxicology methods and educational research, applying a 'hard science' verification technique to improve the quality of data in a domain typically reliant on surveys.

proposals.title: Identifying Protective Factors for Sustained Abstinence from Substance Use Among Student Pharmacists: A Mixed-Methods Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Pharmacy students are a high-risk population for substance use disorders due to stress, access, and knowledge of pharmacology, with most research focusing on risk factors and remediation. A critical gap exists in understanding the factors that promote sustained abstinence or prevent initiation within this same high-stress environment. Identifying these protective factors is essential for developing proactive, strengths-based wellness programs within pharmacy education to bolster student resilience.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, modifiable factors—such as strong professional identity formation, early integration of self-care education, positive peer mentorship, and structured wellness programming—are significantly associated with sustained abstinence from substance use among student pharmacists.
proposals.method: We will employ a convergent parallel mixed-methods design. Quantitatively, a longitudinal cohort of first-year student pharmacists will be surveyed at baseline, 12 months, and 24 months using validated scales for resilience, professional identity, perceived stress, and substance use. Latent class growth analysis will identify trajectories of use/non-use. Qualitatively, in-depth interviews will be conducted with a purposive sample of students from the "sustained abstinence" trajectory to explore reasons for never using or quitting. Qualitative and quantitative data will be integrated during interpretation to develop a comprehensive model of protective factors.
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond deficit models to provide empirical evidence on the protective factors that support student pharmacist well-being. It will directly inform the design of preventive, holistic education programs that reinforce these factors from matriculation. The findings will contribute a novel, positive framework to the pharmacy education literature on student wellness and resilience.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Morello et al. studied the long-term impact of a diabetes education program on student knowledge, this proposal innovatively applies a similar longitudinal, program-evaluation framework to the inverse problem of substance use prevention. It shifts the focus from pathology and risk to identifying sources of strength and resilience within the same educational environment.

proposals.title: A Multi-Institutional, Longitudinal Assessment of a Diabetes Self-Care Education Program on Student Pharmacist Knowledge, Confidence, and Clinical Application
proposals.motivation: Pharmacist-led diabetes management is crucial for patient outcomes, yet the long-term educational impact of single-institution training programs remains unclear due to potential cohort-specific biases. Prior work by Morello et al. (pre-2026) demonstrated sustained benefits from a first-year program at one institution, but generalizability is limited. Replicating this study across diverse pharmacy schools is needed to validate the program's core efficacy and identify contextual factors influencing its success.
proposals.hypothesis: The positive long-term impact (sustained knowledge, confidence, and clinical application) of a structured diabetes self-care education program for student pharmacists is generalizable across different academic institutions and is not solely attributable to cohort-specific characteristics at a single site.
proposals.method: This multi-institutional, longitudinal cohort study will replicate the original single-cohort design across 3-5 geographically and demographically diverse pharmacy schools over 3 academic years. We will employ the same validated pre/post-program and longitudinal follow-up surveys at 6, 12, and 24 months to measure knowledge, confidence (using Likert scales), and self-reported clinical application. Data will be analyzed using mixed-effects models, with institution and cohort year as random effects, to isolate the program's effect from institution- and cohort-specific variance. Subgroup analyses will compare outcomes between institutions and cohorts.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust, generalizable evidence for the effectiveness of integrating hands-on diabetes education early in pharmacy curricula. It will identify best practices and potential moderators (e.g., institutional resources, curricular integration) for program success, offering a scalable model for improving pharmacist competency in diabetes care nationally.
proposals.novelty: While single-cohort educational studies are common, this systematic multi-institutional replication with longitudinal follow-up and mixed-effects modeling to control for cohort and institutional confounders is novel in pharmacy education research, moving beyond proof-of-concept to establish transportable efficacy.

proposals.title: A Randomized Controlled Trial to Disentangle the Therapeutic Components of an Adaptive Closed-Loop Insulin System: The Pharmacist's Role vs. the Algorithm's Adaptation
proposals.motivation: Hybrid closed-loop (HCL) insulin systems, like the Medtronic 670G and Tandem Control-IQ, have improved glycemic control, but their total effect conflates technological efficacy with the human support required for initiation and maintenance. As these systems evolve towards greater autonomy, it is critical to understand the distinct contributions of the adaptive algorithm versus pharmacist-led care to optimize resource allocation and patient outcomes in diabetes management. This addresses a core question in the integration of AI-driven technology and clinical pharmacy expertise.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the glycemic benefits of a next-generation adaptive closed-loop system will be significantly greater when combined with full, expertise-driven pharmacist intervention compared to the system alone or with standardized pharmacist attention, and that the algorithm's adaptation accounts for a larger proportion of the effect than non-specific human support.
proposals.method: We propose a 12-month, three-arm randomized controlled trial in adults with type 1 diabetes. All participants will use an investigational closed-loop system with a model predictive control (MPC) algorithm that adapts insulin pharmacokinetics/pharmacodynamics based on real-time continuous glucose monitor (CGM) trends and meal announcements. Arm 1 receives the system with tech support only. Arm 2 receives the system plus protocol-driven, scheduled pharmacist check-ins. Arm 3 receives the system plus comprehensive, individualized pharmacist intervention (education, data review, and therapy adjustment). The primary outcome is the between-group difference in time-in-range (70-180 mg/dL) over the final 3 months.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical data quantifying the additive value of clinical pharmacist expertise over both technology alone and standardized support within an advanced HCL system. It will inform clinical guidelines on the necessary level of human oversight for optimal system deployment and contribute a novel framework for evaluating 'human-in-the-loop' therapeutic technologies in chronic disease.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have evaluated HCL systems or pharmacist interventions separately, this trial uniquely isolates the specific therapeutic components—adaptive algorithm, non-specific support, and clinical expertise—within a single integrated intervention, a factorial design not previously applied in this context.

proposals.title: A Pragmatic Causal Mediation Trial of Model-Informed Precision Dosing on Long-Term Microvascular Complications in High-Risk Diabetes Patients with Renal Impairment
proposals.motivation: Model-informed precision dosing (MIPD) using pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic (PK/PD) models is a promising strategy to optimize drug exposure, particularly in special populations like patients with diabetes and chronic kidney disease where standard dosing often fails. However, pre-2026 evidence is largely limited to short-term PK endpoints; a direct causal link between achieving PK/PD targets and preventing long-term complications like neuropathy or retinopathy remains unproven. Establishing this link is critical to justify the clinical and educational integration of MIPD programs in pharmacy practice for chronic disease management.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an encouragement intervention for PK/PD-targeted dosing, compared to standard care, will reduce the incidence of long-term microvascular complications over 24 months, and that this treatment effect will be predominantly mediated through improved time-in-therapeutic-range (TITR) for relevant drug exposure metrics, rather than through other care pathways.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 24-month, pragmatic, parallel-arm trial randomizing ~400 high-risk diabetes patients with moderate renal impairment to an encouragement arm for MIPD (using established pre-2026 population PK models for drugs like metformin or sulfonylureas with Bayesian forecasting) or a standard care arm. Primary outcome is a composite of neuropathy progression, retinopathy, or nephropathy, tracked via integrated electronic health records. We will employ causal mediation analysis (using the pre-2026 g-computation formula or structural equation modeling) with TITR as the mediator, adjusting for extensive time-varying covariates (e.g., HbA1c, renal function, adherence from pharmacy claims) to decompose the total treatment effect into direct and indirect (mediated) effects.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first prospective, causal evidence quantifying how much of MIPD's benefit on long-term outcomes is specifically attributable to PK/PD optimization versus concomitant care improvements. It will generate actionable data for health systems on the value of implementing pharmacist-led MIPD services. Furthermore, it will extend the 'All of Us' program's observational findings on long-term medication effects by testing a targeted intervention in a high-risk cohort.
proposals.novelty: While MIPD trials exist, none have used a causal mediation framework to formally isolate and quantify the PK/PD pathway's contribution to long-term clinical endpoints. The pragmatic 'encouragement' design and focus on complication risk, rather than intermediate biomarkers, directly tests the sufficiency of PK/PD optimization as an intervention strategy.

proposals.title: Real-World Mega-Analysis of Inhaled and Oral Insulin Analogs: Associations Between Patient Phenotypes, Glycemic Control, and Hypoglycemia Risk
proposals.motivation: Non-injectable insulin analogs (e.g., inhaled insulin) offer a crucial alternative for patients with needle aversion or lipodystrophy, but their real-world effectiveness and safety across diverse populations remain poorly characterized. Pre-2026, clinical trials are limited in size and duration, and post-marketing studies lack the scale to investigate nuanced interactions with comorbidities and concomitant medications. This gap hinders personalized formulation selection in pharmacy practice, particularly for special populations underrepresented in trials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific real-world patient phenotypes—defined by clusters of respiratory, gastrointestinal, and socioeconomic factors—are significantly associated with differential glycemic outcomes and hypoglycemia risk in users of non-injectable insulin analogs, independent of traditional clinical trial predictors.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort mega-analysis using federated data from the All of Us Research Program and other large EHR networks (e.g., TriNetX), focusing on patients with type 1 or 2 diabetes prescribed inhaled or oral insulin. We will apply a multi-step analytical pipeline: 1) High-dimensional propensity score matching to create comparator cohorts of injectable insulin users. 2) Latent class analysis to identify distinct patient phenotypic clusters. 3) Longitudinal mixed-effects models to compare time-to-event (hypoglycemia) and glycemic variability (coefficient of variation of HbA1c or glucose monitoring data) between clusters and formulations over a 24-month follow-up period.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, real-world evidence on the population-specific performance of non-injectable insulins, directly informing clinical pharmacy decision-making. It will generate actionable phenotypes to guide formulation selection and identify high-risk subgroups requiring enhanced monitoring, thereby addressing a key gap in long-term diabetes management strategies.
proposals.novelty: This moves beyond standard pharmacovigilance by applying advanced phenotyping and mega-analysis techniques specifically to the understudied modality of non-injectable insulin, seeking to explain outcome heterogeneity rather than just reporting aggregate safety signals.

ideas.title: Disentangling the Therapeutic Components of a Closed-Loop Insulin System: The Pharmacist's Role vs. the Algorithm's Adaptation
ideas.description: This research proposes a clinical trial design to evaluate a next-generation, closed-loop insulin system that uses real-time metabolic signals to adapt its pharmacokinetics/pharmacodynamics. Crucially, the study would disentangle the total therapeutic effect by comparing outcomes across three arms: system alone, system with standardized pharmacist attention (protocol-driven check-ins), and system with full, expertise-driven pharmacist intervention. This isolates whether the primary value stems from the adaptive technology, the non-specific benefits of human support, or the specific clinical expertise of the pharmacist, guiding the optimal integration of human and machine intelligence in chronic disease management.

ideas.title: A Stepped-Wedge Cluster Randomized Trial Comparing Pharmacist-Led Diabetes Management to Usual Primary Care
ideas.description: This expanded idea proposes a stepped-wedge cluster randomized trial, drawing an analogy from solutions in public health and implementation science. The analogous problem involves rolling out a new community health program across multiple sites while rigorously evaluating its effect; the solution is the stepped-wedge design, where clusters are randomized to sequentially transition from control to intervention. Adapted to this context, the research would involve multiple primary care practices. These practices would be randomized to different start times for implementing the pharmacist-led diabetes management clinic. Data on patient outcomes (e.g., HbA1c, hospitalizations, quality of life) would be collected continuously across all phases. This design allows for a robust comparison to usual primary care by providing both concurrent controls (practices not yet transitioned) and historical controls (the same practice's performance before implementation), while ensuring all participating practices and their patients eventually benefit from the pharmacist-led model.

ideas.title: Real-World Mega-Analysis of Non-Injectable Insulin Analog PK/PD to Decode Population-Specific Efficacy and Safety
ideas.description: This research direction leverages aggregated electronic health record data from millions of patients to perform mega-analyses on the pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics of inhaled and oral insulin analogs. By applying large-scale data science techniques to this specific modality, the project aims to uncover novel and nuanced associations between a wide array of real-world covariates—such as respiratory comorbidities, smoking status, GI disorders, concomitant medications, and socioeconomic factors—and critical outcomes like glycemic control variability, hypoglycemia risk, and treatment persistence. This approach moves beyond the limitations of clinical trial populations to generate evidence on how these alternative insulin formulations perform across the full heterogeneity of special populations in routine care, ultimately enabling truly personalized formulation selection.

ideas.title: Cost-effectiveness analysis of the clinic model using a Value Engineering framework
ideas.description: This research adapts the Value Engineering (VE) framework from industrial engineering and manufacturing to evaluate the economic impact of the pharmacist-led collaborative care clinic model. The analogous solution involves systematically analyzing a product's functions to eliminate unnecessary costs without sacrificing performance. Adapted to this context, the research will decompose the clinical intervention into its constituent functional processes, mapping the cost of each process against its quantifiable contribution to patient health outcomes. This will create a detailed cost-to-value profile, enabling the identification of high-expenditure, low-impact activities for targeted optimization, thereby providing a novel, function-based methodology for determining the model's true cost-effectiveness and guiding its most efficient design.

ideas.title: Mechanistic Longitudinal Cohort with Embedded Decline Analysis to Unpack Sustainability of Glycemic Control
ideas.description: This study actively investigates the sustainability of adherence and glycemic improvements over 24 months by mapping trajectories against hypothesized behavioral, social, and clinical mechanisms assessed at scheduled intervals. Instead of merely measuring final outcomes, it uses a mixed-methods approach (e.g., ecological momentary assessment, qualitative interviews, clinical data) to understand why outcomes are sustained or not. A key innovation is a pre-defined, triggered sub-study that enrolls participants showing significant decline into an intensive 'rescue' protocol, combining deep-dive contextual analysis with targeted support to elucidate real-time barriers to sustainability. This design transforms a durability check into a mechanism-learning engine, generating actionable knowledge for designing future interventions.

ideas.title: Calibrating Self-Reported Adherence with Objective Measures Using Ecological Momentary Assessment
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that self-reported adherence is sufficiently accurate by proposing a calibration method adapted from environmental psychology. In that field, researchers overcame the unreliability of global self-reports for pro-environmental behaviors by using Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) to collect real-time self-reports via smartphone, then calibrating them against objective sensor data to identify when self-reports were valid. Adapted to diabetes research, the study would have patients use a smartphone app for 2-4 weeks to provide real-time, context-tagged reports of medication-taking, while simultaneously collecting objective adherence data via electronic monitors or pharmacy claims. The core analysis would use calibration modeling to identify the specific conditions (e.g., patient characteristics, social contexts, times of day) under which self-reported adherence measures are sufficiently accurate predictors of HbA1c outcomes, potentially creating a validated, context-aware self-report instrument for clinical use.

ideas.title: Causal Mediation Trial of PK/PD-Targeted Dosing on Long-Term Complications in Special Populations
ideas.description: This study implements a prospective, pragmatic trial to isolate the causal effect of improved PK/PD profiles on long-term clinical outcomes. Special populations are randomized to an encouragement arm for model-informed precision dosing or a standard care arm, with outcomes tracked via integrated health records. The design incorporates extensive baseline and time-varying covariate collection to control for confounding and uses causal mediation analysis to quantify the proportion of the treatment effect explained by PK/PD improvement versus other pathways. This approach directly tests whether optimizing PK/PD is a sufficient intervention to modify complication risk.

ideas.title: Longitudinal Ecological Succession Model for Sustained Glycemic Control
ideas.description: This idea adapts the long-term ecological monitoring framework used to study ecosystem succession and stability. In ecology, understanding long-term recovery requires tracking systems through multiple phases beyond initial re-growth. Analogously, to assess if improvements in diabetes adherence and glycemic control are truly sustained, the study must extend to one year or more and be designed to capture the different phases of behavioral maintenance. The adapted research idea is a longitudinal, multi-phase cohort study. It would follow participants at 12, 18, and 24 months post-intervention, measuring core outcomes (e.g., HbA1c, adherence metrics) alongside behavioral and psychological indicators of habit stability and resilience to challenges. This design moves beyond a simple extended endpoint to model how the 'system' of diabetes self-management evolves, stabilizes, or degrades over time, identifying critical junctures for sustained success.

ideas.title: Proactive Deployment of Physiology-Adaptive Insulins for At-Risk Populations
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a paradigm shift from reactive diabetes management to proactive metabolic health preservation. It combines a system for proactively identifying individuals at high risk for insulin dysregulation (e.g., via predictive analytics on EHR and continuous biomarker data) with the deployment of next-generation 'smart' insulins engineered to respond to real-time metabolic signals. The synergy enables intervention during the critical pre-diabetic or early dysglycemic phase, where traditional referral pathways are absent. By using a physiology-adaptive therapeutic as the primary tool, the approach aims to normalize metabolic trajectories in a targeted, personalized manner at the population level, potentially preventing the onset of overt type 2 diabetes and its complications.

ideas.title: Challenge PK/PD sufficiency via ecological endpoint validation
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that improved pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic (PK/PD) profiles are sufficient to predict reduced long-term complications in special populations. It draws an analogy from environmental toxicology, where standard lab bioassays (analogous to PK/PD measures) were found insufficient for predicting ecosystem health, leading to the development of 'mesocosm' studies—bridging experiments that test chemical effects in semi-natural systems. The adapted research idea is to design 'clinical mesocosm' studies: controlled, longitudinal cohorts within special populations (e.g., elderly with polypharmacy, renal impairment patients) where optimized drug regimens are implemented. The cohort is treated as a closed system with intensive, multi-dimensional monitoring beyond standard PK/PD to directly measure the emergence or suppression of long-term complications, thereby testing the sufficiency of PK/PD improvement as a surrogate for real-world clinical benefit.

ideas.title: Causal PK/PD-Glycemia Linkage via Controlled Challenge Integration with CGM Analytics
ideas.description: This research employs a controlled crossover study design to establish causal links between pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic (PK/PD) variations and glycemic outcomes. Participants undergo standardized metabolic challenges (e.g., meal tolerance tests, exercise) while undergoing continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) and frequent blood sampling for drug/metabolite concentration and pharmacodynamic biomarker assessment. Advanced time-series analysis, including cross-correlation and system identification techniques, is applied to the synchronized high-resolution CGM and PK/PD data. This approach isolates the specific impact of PK/PD variability on real-time glycemia from confounding behavioral factors, enabling the development of robust, mechanistic models for personalized therapy.

ideas.title: Causal Mediation Analysis of Adherence Pathways on Clinical Markers
ideas.description: This research adapts the 'load path analysis' method from structural engineering to clinical epidemiology. In engineering, the impact of a material's strength on a structure's final performance is understood by modeling the forces through specific components. Analogously, we propose using causal mediation analysis to examine the relationship between improved medication adherence and secondary clinical outcomes like blood pressure or lipid levels. The study would quantitatively decompose the total effect of an adherence intervention into direct effects and indirect effects transmitted through key mediating pathways (e.g., pharmacokinetic consistency, reduced vascular stress, concomitant lifestyle changes), thereby identifying the primary biological and behavioral mechanisms by which adherence exerts its influence on specific markers.

ideas.title: Auditing for Equity: A Fairness-First Framework for Proactive Patient Identification
ideas.description: This research tests a proactive identification system for at-risk patients that is explicitly designed to maximize equitable impact. The core intervention integrates continuous algorithmic fairness audits, using multiple models and subgroup analysis to detect and correct for bias in risk prediction. The system includes a feedback mechanism where identification performance and patient outcomes are monitored across demographic strata, and the enrollment protocol is adaptively adjusted. The primary hypothesis is that this fairness-by-design approach will yield greater and more equitable population health improvement compared to a standard proactive model or a referral-only baseline.

ideas.title: Model-Informed Precision Dosing (MIPD) for Insulin: From Covariate Quantification to Variability Minimization
ideas.description: This research integrates the robust covariate quantification of antibiotic-style population PK modeling with an explicit optimization goal of minimizing insulin PK/PD variability. The core methodology involves developing a nonlinear mixed-effects model for insulin analogs that identifies key demographic, physiological, and genetic covariates influencing exposure. Crucially, this model is then used in-silico to simulate and iteratively refine personalized dosing algorithms (e.g., adaptive, covariate-adjusted regimens) with the primary objective of minimizing both inter- and intra-individual variability in key PK/PD metrics. The final output is a set of clinically actionable, model-informed dosing strategies ready for prospective validation, shifting the research endpoint from mere variability measurement to its direct, model-driven reduction.

ideas.title: Causal Mediation Analysis of Insulin Analog PK/PD: Disentangling Ancestry, Physiology, and Environment
ideas.description: This study moves beyond correlating PK/PD with race/ethnicity by identifying and quantifying the specific causal pathways that lead to population differences. A diverse cohort is deeply phenotyped for genetic ancestry, body composition, biomarkers, lifestyle, and socio-environmental factors. Using a causal mediation analysis framework, the research measures how much of any observed between-group variation in insulin exposure and response is directly explained by these mechanistic mediators, rather than by the social category itself. The outcome is a predictive, mechanistic model of insulin pharmacokinetics grounded in biology and modifiable environment, not race.

ideas.title: Mechanistic PBPK/PD Platform to Predict and Personalize Insulin Analog Therapy Across Patient Subpopulations
ideas.description: This research integrates systematic clinical data on differential variable effects with a mechanistic PBPK/PD modeling framework. The synergy lies in using the clinical data to rigorously parameterize and validate the PBPK models for each insulin analog under specific pathophysiological conditions. This validated platform can then perform in-silico experiments to predict PK/PD outcomes for untested patient subpopulations and clinical scenarios, generating hypotheses for optimal analog selection and dosing. The ultimate output is a clinically actionable, simulation-based tool for personalizing insulin therapy, moving beyond descriptive observation to predictive, mechanism-based guidance.

ideas.title: Ethnicity-specific PK/PD of inhaled/oral insulin analogs: bridging formulation and population pharmacology
ideas.description: This research will systematically investigate the pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics of inhaled and oral insulin analogs across diverse, well-defined ethnic populations. It aims to identify and quantify how population-specific factors—such as nasal/lung physiology, gastrointestinal environment, and genetic polymorphisms affecting mucosal absorption and first-pass metabolism—influence the bioavailability and action profiles of these alternative formulations. The study moves beyond comparing ethnic groups for a single drug to elucidating the mechanistic interplay between formulation route and intrinsic ethnic factors, thereby generating essential data to guide the development of tailored dosing regimens and potentially inform the design of next-generation, population-optimized non-injectable insulins.

ideas.title: Adaptive Microdose-Escalation for Validated Personalization of PK/PD
ideas.description: This redesigned system overcomes the extrapolation flaw by combining initial microdose mapping with controlled, real-time dose escalation. A sensitive assay establishes a baseline PK profile from a subtherapeutic dose, which is immediately fed into a Bayesian pharmacokinetic model. The model then guides a safe, single-session escalation to a low therapeutic dose, with continuous biosampling to measure the actual PK/PD relationship at the relevant dose. This closed-loop approach validates the predictive power of the microdose data for each individual, ensuring the personalized model is calibrated to therapeutic exposure levels.

ideas.title: Adapt Pharmacokinetic Landscape Mapping from Oncology to Insulin Injection Site Dynamics
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution of 'pharmacokinetic landscape mapping' from oncology drug delivery to the problem of comparing insulin analog action profiles across injection sites. In oncology, this approach systematically models how drug pharmacokinetics vary based on administration route and local tissue microenvironment, integrating factors like blood flow and tissue density. Adapted to insulin research, the study would move beyond simple comparison of observed absorption rates for rapid- and long-acting analogs. Instead, it would construct a predictive, quantitative model that links measurable local tissue properties (e.g., subcutaneous adipose tissue thickness, vascularization, metabolic activity) at standard injection sites (abdomen, thigh, arm) to the resulting pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic profiles. This framework would explain the mechanistic basis for site-to-site variability and could ultimately inform personalized injection site recommendations based on an individual's specific tissue characteristics.

ideas.title: Causal Inference for Diabetes Complication Prevention Using Target Trial Emulation
ideas.description: This study uses a target trial emulation framework to rigorously estimate the causal effect of an intervention on specific microvascular complications. It structures analysis of longitudinal clinical data to mimic a randomized trial, defining clear eligibility, treatment strategies, and follow-up start points. Advanced causal inference methods are applied to adjust for baseline and time-varying confounders, providing a robust estimate of the intervention's effect on time-to-event outcomes like retinopathy or neuropathy diagnosis.

ideas.title: Federated Causal Inference Platform for Robust Real-World Pharmacometric Discovery
ideas.description: This system establishes a privacy-preserving, federated network for conducting rigorous causal analyses on real-world EHR data. Instead of simple mega-analysis, it uses standardized computational phenotyping and federated learning to apply advanced causal inference methods (e.g., high-dimensional propensity score adjustment) locally within each participating healthcare system. The platform centrally synthesizes only debiased effect estimates and diagnostic metrics, enforcing pre-specified causal criteria to distinguish plausible mechanistic relationships from spurious correlations. This approach transforms large-scale EHR data into reliable evidence on covariate-drug response relationships by directly addressing and mitigating the fundamental sources of bias inherent in observational data aggregation.

ideas.title: Adaptive, Real-Time Personalization of Basal-Bolus Therapy Using Continuous Learning from Multimodal Data
ideas.description: This research develops a closed-loop learning system for insulin dosing in special populations that moves beyond static algorithms. It integrates continuous glucose monitoring, wearable data, and episodic insulin pharmacokinetic measurements into a Bayesian probabilistic model to estimate individual, time-varying parameters for insulin sensitivity and carbohydrate ratio. The system provides adaptive dosing recommendations that are updated daily based on recent patient-specific data, and it includes explicit safety constraints and uncertainty quantification to guard against extreme recommendations. This approach aims to create a personalized, responsive dosing assistant that safely manages intra-individual metabolic variability.

ideas.title: A Federated Meta-Analysis Framework for Privacy-Preserving Pharmacometric Mega-Analyses
ideas.description: This idea adapts the federated data solution from large-scale particle physics collaborations to the challenge of scaling pharmacometric analyses to mega-analyses of real-world evidence. In physics, projects like the LHC use a worldwide computing grid where data is processed locally at partner institutions using common standards, and only aggregated results are combined centrally. Adapted to pharmacometrics, this means creating a secure, federated network where each healthcare institution runs a standardized pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic (PK/PD) model search pipeline on its local EHR data within its own firewall. The central research entity coordinates the study design, distributes the identical software container, and receives only the summarized statistical outputs (e.g., covariate effect sizes, confidence intervals, model fit indices) from each node. These results are then meta-analyzed to discover novel covariate-outcome associations across a population of millions, solving the scaling problem while adhering to data privacy regulations and institutional data sovereignty concerns.

ideas.title: Apply behavioral economics nudges

ideas.sourceTitle: Improved Patient-Reported Medication Adherence, Patient Satisfaction, and Glycemic Control in a Collaborative Care Pharmacist-Led Diabetes “Tune-Up” Clinic

ideas.title: Adaptive Insulin Analogue System: Closed-Loop, Population-Informed, Real-Time Titration
ideas.description: This system moves beyond designing a single static analog for a broad population. It combines a small, purpose-engineered library of insulin analogs with distinct pharmacokinetic and clearance profiles with an advanced algorithmic delivery platform. The system employs continuous physiological monitoring and adaptive, individualized pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic modeling to dynamically select and titrate the most appropriate analog in real-time. This approach directly addresses the critical flaw of inter-patient variability by providing a personalized, adaptive therapeutic regimen that maintains stability even as a patient's metabolic state changes, such as during fluctuations in renal function or inflammatory status.

ideas.title: Mechanistic Disconnect: Testing PK/PD Surrogacy Against Organ-Specific Complication Trajectories in Special Populations
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a longitudinal study in special populations (e.g., elderly, those with renal impairment) to determine if an intervention's optimized pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic (PK/PD) profile correlates with a slowing of the specific, early pathophysiological processes leading to distinct complications like retinopathy or neuropathy. It moves beyond asking if improved PK/PD reduces complication rates, to testing how and in which tissue beds the improved drug exposure disrupts disease progression. The synergy lies in using high-resolution, complication-specific biomarkers and imaging to challenge the assumption that a 'better' PK/PD profile uniformly benefits all microvascular beds, potentially revealing organ-specific surrogacy gaps that inform targeted drug development and personalized monitoring strategies.

ideas.title: Scale up for mass production and global access
ideas.description: Redesign complex delivery systems for low-cost, high-volume manufacturing to improve global diabetes care equity.
ideas.sourceTitle: Diabetes type 1 and type 2—Insulin delivery systems

ideas.title: Adaptive Dosing Algorithms for Special Populations Using Ecological Niche Modeling Principles
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from ecological niche modeling (Species Distribution Models) to the problem of tailoring insulin dosing for special populations. In ecology, SDMs integrate multi-dimensional environmental data to predict species habitat suitability. Analogously, we propose developing a dosing algorithm that integrates a patient's multi-dimensional clinical profile (renal/hepatic function, body composition, age, metabolic markers) as the 'niche.' The model would be trained on analog PK/PD data and clinical outcomes from heterogeneous cohorts to learn the complex interactions between these parameters and insulin requirements. For a new patient, the algorithm would map their specific 'physiological niche' to a predicted optimal dosing regimen, providing a truly tailored, adaptive approach for managing diabetes in patients with renal/hepatic impairment, obesity, or advanced age.

ideas.title: Personalized Nanodosing Trials to Map Drug Exposure and Early Biomarker Response for Diabetic Complications
ideas.description: This research direction proposes using microdosing of investigational drugs (e.g., SGLT2 inhibitors, GLP-1 RAs, novel agents) combined with ultrasensitive assays (accelerator mass spectrometry, single-molecule arrays) to define individual PK variability in patients with diabetes. Concurrently, it employs high-sensitivity, complication-specific biomarkers (like proteomic signatures of extracellular matrix turnover for neuropathy or optical coherence tomography angiography metrics for retinopathy) as PD endpoints. By correlating an individual's unique drug exposure profile with the magnitude and timing of early biomarker modulation, the study aims to build predictive models of long-term complication risk reduction. This allows for the rapid, safe stratification of patients to the most effective drug for preventing their specific complication trajectory, transforming complication management from reactive to preemptive and personalized.

ideas.title: Causal Variance Component Targeting: A Mechanism-Based Framework to Identify and Modulate Specific Sources of PK/PD Variability
ideas.description: This research program establishes a causal engineering framework to systematically reduce pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic variability. It begins by using quantitative systems pharmacology and mixed-effects models to partition total observed variability into distinct, attributable components from genetic, physiological, environmental, and drug product sources. The core hypothesis is that specific, modifiable variance components can be selectively targeted and minimized through tailored interventions, such as precision dosing algorithms, adjunct therapies, or smart drug delivery systems. Success is measured by a statistically significant reduction in the magnitude of a pre-specified variance component in a controlled experiment, moving beyond measurement to causal variance modulation.

ideas.title: Proactive Seismic Retrofitting for At-Risk Patients
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that healthcare models must wait for patient referral. Drawing an analogy from structural engineering's approach to earthquake preparedness, where predictive models identify and retrofit vulnerable buildings proactively, this research adapts that solution. It proposes developing a predictive 'clinical seismic risk' model to identify at-risk patients before a major health event, then proactively enrolling them into targeted preventive interventions. The adapted research idea is to test whether this model-driven, pre-emptive identification and enrollment strategy yields a greater population health impact (preventing the 'collapse' of health) compared to the traditional, reactive model that only acts upon referral.

ideas.title: Adaptive Digital Twin for Insulin Pharmacokinetics/Pharmacodynamics via PBPK Modeling with Real-Time Data Assimilation
ideas.description: This research develops a personalized, adaptive digital twin for insulin therapy by coupling a mechanistic PBPK/PD model with a real-time data assimilation layer. The system continuously ingests data from CGM and wearable sensors (e.g., heart rate, bioimpedance) to estimate and update patient-specific physiological parameters, such as local blood flow and insulin sensitivity, through Bayesian filtering. This closed-loop calibration ensures the model dynamically reflects the individual's current pathophysiological state, enabling accurate, real-time simulation of insulin absorption and action. The validated system aims to provide a robust platform for predicting personalized insulin dosing and optimizing automated insulin delivery algorithms under varying conditions.

ideas.title: Personalized PK/PD Mapping via Microfluidic Single-Cell Pharmacoscopy
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from astrophysics, where high-precision instruments extract universe-scale information from faint, remote signals. The analogous problem in pharmacology is the need to understand an individual's full pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic (PK/PD) profile without exposing them to risky therapeutic doses or lengthy trial phases. The adapted solution is a 'pharmacoscopy' platform. A patient-derived cell population is subjected to nanoscale or microdoses of a drug within a controlled microfluidic environment. Ultra-sensitive, single-cell resolution assays (e.g., next-generation mass spectrometry, digital PCR) function as the pharmacological 'telescope,' detecting the earliest, faintest molecular signatures of drug action, metabolism, and cellular response. This high-resolution, multi-omic snapshot is then computationally integrated with prior mechanistic knowledge into a personalized systems pharmacology model. The model infers the individual's likely full-scale PK curve, therapeutic window, and potential for adverse events, effectively mapping their personalized drug response landscape from a single, rapid, and safe microdose experiment.

ideas.title: Adapting Robust Control Theory from Engineering to Actively Minimize PK/PD Variability
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from control systems engineering, specifically robust control theory. In that field, the solution to unpredictable system behavior is not just to measure the variability but to design controllers that actively compensate for disturbances, minimizing output variability. Adapted to pharmacology, the research focuses on developing 'adaptive therapeutic systems' where a primary therapeutic agent is paired with a control element designed to sense and counteract key physiological sources of PK/PD variability. The primary endpoint shifts from characterizing variability to demonstrating its active reduction, aiming for drug-response profiles that are robust and consistent across a diverse patient population.

ideas.title: Adapting Environmental Contaminant Fate Modeling for Personalized Insulin PBPK
ideas.description: This idea expands the original PBPK modeling concept by drawing an analogy from environmental science. In environmental toxicology, the fate and transport of contaminants are predicted using sophisticated, spatially aware multi-compartment models that account for the chemical's properties and the heterogeneity of the environment. This solution is adapted to pharmacokinetics by conceptualizing the human body as a heterogeneous 'landscape' for insulin. The adapted research idea involves building next-generation PBPK models that explicitly simulate insulin's journey from subcutaneous injection through spatially defined physiological compartments, such as the injection site microenvironment, regional lymphatics, and organ-specific capillary densities. By integrating patient-specific pathophysiological 'landscape' data—like local tissue health, perfusion maps, and interstitial matrix properties—the model can simulate highly personalized insulin absorption and action, providing a powerful tool for optimizing diabetes therapy in conditions like lipodystrophy, heart failure, or obesity.

ideas.title: Apply ecological population modeling to insulin pharmacokinetics
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to a parallel problem in ecology—estimating animal population dynamics from sparse, noisy field data. Ecologists successfully use hierarchical Bayesian state-space models to integrate disparate data sources and quantify how environmental covariates affect demographic rates. We adapt this solution to insulin analog pharmacokinetics (PK). A hierarchical Bayesian nonlinear mixed-effects model will be developed, treating sparse clinical PK/glucose data as observations of a latent PK 'population.' This framework will explicitly quantify uncertainty and estimate the effects of key patient covariates (e.g., BMI, renal function, age, ethnicity) on insulin PK parameters, providing robust, probabilistic descriptions of dose-exposure relationships across diverse global populations, analogous to how ecologists model populations across diverse habitats.

ideas.title: Context-Aware, Tunable Insulin Analog for Pathophysiological Subpopulations
ideas.description: This research direction aims to develop a single, multi-functional insulin analog whose action profile can be switched between a stable basal state and a rapid bolus state via a simple, patient-administered trigger (e.g., a co-injected small molecule, light, or ultrasound). Crucially, the molecular engineering will use an inverse problem approach from the outset: the analog's structure, trigger mechanism, and formulation will be explicitly designed to ensure reliable, predictable tuning and stable pharmacokinetics despite the pathophysiological challenges of specific high-risk populations, such as severe obesity (with altered subcutaneous dynamics and insulin resistance) or renal failure (with reduced clearance). This results in a personalized medicine platform where the same core therapeutic agent can be safely and effectively adapted to an individual's daily needs and their unique physiological context.
